questions.microTopic: Physiography of India (Location, Division, Physiographic Regions)
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements with regard to
the Islands in India:
I. - The Lakshadweep Islands were formed as a
result of volcanic eruptions of the Reunion
Hotspot.
II. 	 The Andaman and Nicobar Islands constitute
a part of the Arakan Yoma range.
III. 	 Indira Col, the southernmost point of India,
is located on the Great Nicobar Island.
Which of the statements given above is/are
correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The Andaman & Nicobar Islands and the Lakshadweep are the two main island groups in India.
● Lakshadweep is situated in the Arabian Sea. These are scattered between 8°N-12°N and 71°E -74°E longitude. All these islands are very small in size. The largest island among these, the Minicoy, only has an area of 4.5 square km.
○ **The entire island group of** **Lakshadweep is built of coral** **deposits.** All these islands are of coral origin. They have been built up by corals, the microscopic polyps.
○ **The Lakshadweep are formed by coral deposits on undersea mountains formed by** **eruption from Reunion Volcanic Hotspot.** Around 66 million years ago, an eruption from a volcanic hotspot near modern-day Africa, known as the Réunion Hotspot, led to massive lava flows, creating underwater mountains and ridges across the Indian Ocean. With time, these volcanic seamounts became habitat of tiny marine organisms known as corals. They began growing and built massive reef structures over millennia. **So, statement I is correct.**
● The island group of Andaman and Nicobar is located in the Bay of Bengal. It is situated roughly between 6°N-14°N and 92°E -94°E. It is divided into two broad categories – the Andaman in the north and the Nicobar in the south. They are separated by a waterbody which is called the Ten degree channel. The Barren Island in the Andamans is India's only active volcano.
○ The Andaman and Nicobar islands are mostly elevated portions of submarine mountains. Some islands in this group are also of volcanic origin. The Andaman and Nicobar islands (A&N) are **the** **emergent peaks of a submerged mountain range related to the Arakan Yoma** of mainland Myanmar. Arakan Yoma is an **extension of the Himalayas located in Myanmar.** **So, statement II is correct.**
● The Nicobar Islands include Car Nicobar, Little Nicobar, and Great Nicobar Islands from north to south. The **Southernmost point of India is Indira Point.** Indira Point is **situated in the Great Nicobar** **Island**. It is situated at  6°45’38” N latitude. It is not visible today because it was submerged under the sea in the 2004 Tsunami.
○ **Indira Col is India’s northernmost point**. It has an altitude of 5,764 metres and is located in the Siachen Muztagh in the Karakoram Range. **So, statement III is not correct.** **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**
**Knowledge Box**
● The western most point of India is Ghuar Moti, which lies in the Kutch district of Gujarat.
● The easternmost point of India is Kibithu, which lies in Arunachal Pradesh.
● The southernmost point of mainland India is Kanyakumari in Tamilnadu.
● The northernmost point of the Indian mainland is Indira Col. New Moore Island or Purbasha, was an island in the Bay of Bengal. It has now ceased to exist. In the early 1980s, both Bangladesh and India staked claim to the island.

questions.subject: Geography
questions.questionText:
With reference to La Niña, consider the following
statements:
I. - During this event, the thermocline deepens in
the Western Pacific and becomes shallower
in the Eastern Pacific.
II. 	 During La Niña years, below-normal
temperatures are generally observed over
India during the winter season.
III. 	 La Niña Modoki is marked by cooling in the
central Pacific, accompanied by warming in
both the eastern and western Pacific Ocean.
Which of the statements given above is/are
correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **La Nina** is the **cold phase of the El Nino–Southern Oscillation (ENSO)** phenomenon. It is marked by **cooler-than-normal sea surface temperatures (SSTs)** in the **central and eastern** **equatorial Pacific Ocean**, caused by **stronger-than-usual trade winds** that enhance **upwelling** of cold, nutrient-rich water.
● The thermocline is the oceanic transition layer separating warm surface waters from colder deep waters.
○ During this event, easterly trade winds strengthen significantly, pushing large volumes of warm surface water westward toward Indonesia and Australia. This accumulation of warm water causes the **thermocline to deepen in the western Pacific.** ○ As surface waters are driven away from the South American coast (Peru–Ecuador), cold, nutrientrich deep water rises to the surface through upwelling, resulting in a **shallower thermocline** **in the eastern Pacific. So, statement I is correct.**
● In the **Indian context**, La Nina typically leads to **normal or above-normal rainfall** during the southwest monsoon season. However, during La Nina years, **below-normal surface temperatures** are generally recorded during the **winter season** over much of India, due to the **enhanced cold air** **circulation** patterns linked to the phenomenon. **So, statement II is correct.**
● **La Nina Modoki:** This is the opposite of El Nino Modoki.  It is marked by **cooling in the central** **Pacific**, accompanied by **warming in both the eastern and western Pacific Ocean. So,** **statement III is correct.**
● **El Nino Modoki:** This is a distinct phenomenon where there is strong anomalous warming in the central tropical Pacific, flanked by **anomalously cool regions in both the eastern and western tropical** **Pacific.** The term "Modoki" essentially means "similar but different" or "a different kind." **Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.**
**Knowledge Box**
**El Nino–Southern Oscillation (ENSO):**
● Naturally occurring, **large-scale climate phenomenon** involving fluctuating ocean temperatures in the **central and eastern equatorial Pacific.**
● It occurs in an irregular cycle, typically **every 2 to 7 years.**
● **It has three phases:** ○ **El Nino (Warm Phase):** ■ **warmer-than-average SSTs** in the central and eastern tropical Pacific Ocean. ■ **East-to-west trade winds weaken** -> warm water pool in the western Pacific (like
Indonesia and Australia) shifts eastward -> higher-than-average pressure over Indonesia and Australia & Low pressure over the eastern Pacific. ■ **drought** in regions like **Indonesia, India, and northern Australia.** ■ **heavy rainfall and flooding** in parts of western South America. ○ **La Nina (Cool Phase):** ■ **cooler-than-average SSTs** in the central and eastern tropical Pacific Ocean. ■ **East-to-west trade winds strengthen ->** more warm water near Indonesia and
Australia i.e. Low pressure conditions & high pressure over the eastern pacific. ■ **increased rainfall and flooding** in countries like Indonesia, Malaysia, and Australia.
India also. ■ **drought conditions** in the southern US and some parts of South America. ■ below-normal surface temperatures during **winters in India.** ○ **ENSO-Neutral (Transition Phase):** Neither El Nino nor La Nina. This phase often acts as a transition period between El Nino and La Nina.
**Relevance:** The India Meteorological Department (IMD) has forecasted above-normal rainfall for the upcoming monsoon season in India, with “favourable” La Niña conditions likely to develop by August–September.

questions.microTopic: Physiography of India (Location, Division, Physiographic Regions)
questions.questionText:
With regard to the Trans-Himalayas, consider the
following statements:
I. - The Zanskar, Ladakh and Kailash ranges are
part of the Trans-Himalayas.
II. 	 Siachen Glacier is located in the Ladakh
Range.
III.	 In India, the trans-Himalayas are spread over
Ladakh and Jammu & Kashmir only.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The Mountain ranges situated to the north of the Great Himalayan range are called Trans-Himalayas. It runs parallel to the Great Himalayan range for a distance of about 1000 km in the eastwest direction. Its average elevation is about 3000 metres above mean sea level.
● **Zanskar,** **Ladakh,** Karakoram **and Kailash are** **the** **main** **Trans-** **Himalayan** **ranges**. **So,** **statement I is correct.**
○ The northernmost range of the Trans-Himalaya in India is the **Karakoram range.** ○ **The Ladakh Range** lies to the **south of the Karakoram**. It **lies between the valleys of** **the Indus and the Shyok rivers.** ○ **The Zaskar Range** lies to the **south of the Ladakh Range**. It runs parallel to the Great Himalayas, extending southeast from the Suru River to the Upper Karnali River. Kamet Peak (25,446 ft) is the highest peak in this range.
● The **Siachen Glacier is located in the Karakoram range**. The Siachen Glacier is the longest glacier in the Karakoram Range. It is also the longest glacier in India. **statement II is not correct.**
● Trans-Himalayan area is found in India in **Jammu & Kashmir, Ladakh** and the **Spiti region of** **Himachal Pradesh. So, statement III is not correct.** **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Geography
questions.questionText:
With reference to the rock systems of India,
consider the following statements:
**Statement I:**
The Dharwar is economically one of the most
important rock systems of India.
**Statement II:**
The Dharwar rocks contain huge deposits of
cement-grade limestone.
Which one of the following is correct in respect of
the statements given above?

options.a:
Both Statement I and Statement II are correct
and Statement II explains Statement I
options.b:
Both Statement I and Statement II are correct
but Statement II does **not** explain Statement I
options.c:
Statement I is correct but Statement II is
incorrect
options.d:
Statement I is incorrect but Statement II is
correct

questions.explanationMarkdown:
● Geologically India is divided into multiple rock strata which were formed at different stages of India's geological history. The **Dharwar System** of rocks was formed between 3100 to 1000 million years ago. These are the oldest metamorphic rocks of India.  They are formed due to the metamorphosis of the sediments formed out of the Archean rocks.
○ They are mainly found in the Aravallis, Chotanagpur plateau, Meghalaya, Karnataka and Tamil Nadu.
● **The Dharwar system of rocks is economically the most important rock system of India.** It is because **they are rich in metallic minerals like iron ore, copper, gold, Manganese, etc.** They are rich in metallic minerals such as iron, manganese, gold, copper etc. They are considered to be the storehouses of metallic minerals and hence have a **high economic significance. So, statement -I is** **correct but statement -II is incorrect.**
**Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**
**Knowledge Box**
Archean, Dharwar, Purana, Dravidian and Aryan are the major rock systems of India.
● **The Archean rock system** is the oldest rock system of India dating back to about 4 billion years (pre-Cambrian rocks). They serve as the basement rocks for other rock systems. They are found in the Aravalli hills, Deccan plateau and the northeast of India. They mostly contain gneisses (granite, gabbro etc.) and schists (crystalline rocks such as mica, chlorite, talc etc.). These rocks are formed when magma underneath the earth's surface solidifies and hence they are devoid of any fossils. These rocks are abundant in metallic as well as non-metallic minerals such as iron, manganese, copper, bauxite, gold, lead, mica, graphite etc.
● **The Cuddapah and Vindhyan rock** systems are together known as the **Purana** **rock system**. They are formed by the erosion and deposition of Archean and Dharwar rocks. The process is believed to have taken place between 1400-600 million years ago. They are mostly sedimentary in nature. They are poor in metallic minerals.
○ **Large deposits of cement grade limestones are present in Cuddapah** **rocks.** ○ Diamond-bearing regions are present in the Vindhyan rock system.
● **The Dravidian rock system** was formed around 600-300 million years ago. Dravidian rocks are mostly found in the extra-Peninsular regions of the Himalayas and the Gangetic plain.
○ Dravidian rocks include shales, sandstones, clays, quartzites, slates, salts, talc, dolomite, marble, etc. ○ They are abundant in fossils, and this period saw the beginning of coal formation.
● **The Aryan Rock System** is made up of diverse kinds of rocks. It comprises Gondwana rock system, Jurassic system, Deccan trap and Tertiary rocks.
○ Gondwana rocks contain nearly 98% of India’s coal reserves. They also have rich deposits of iron ore, copper, uranium, and antimony. These are sedimentary deposits. ○ Weathering of Deccan Trap rocks for a long time has given birth to black cotton soil known as ‘regur.’ ○ The tertiary rock system belongs to the Cenozoic era, formed around 60 million years ago. Important rock systems include Karewas of Kashmir, Bhangra and Khadar of the Gangetic plains etc.

questions.subject: Agriculture
questions.questionText:
With reference to oilseed production in India, consider the following statements:
I. - India is the fourth largest producer of oilseeds globally.
II. 	 More than 50% of India’s edible oil demand is met through imports.
III. 	 Oilseeds hold the second-highest acreage and production value in Indian agriculture after food grains.
IV. 	 Rajasthan, Madhya Pradesh, Gujarat, and Maharashtra together contribute over 75% of India’s total oilseed output.
Which of the statements given above are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Oilseeds** are among the most **important agricultural** **commodities after food grains** in India, forming a vital component of the country’s **agri-economy** and export basket. **Major oilseeds cultivated include** groundnut, soybean, rapeseed-mustard, sunflower, sesame, safflower, and niger seed.
● **India is the fourth-largest producer** of oilseeds globally, accounting for about **5–6% of world production.** Despite having nearly **20.8% of the total cultivated area** under oilseeds, productivity levels remain modest due to **rainfed cultivation** across nearly 72% of the sown area. **So, statement I is correct.**
● **Oilseed crops** are the **second most** important component of the agricultural economy after cereals (food grains), in terms of both **area under cultivation and value** **of output.**  **So, statement III is correct.**
● The major oilseed-producing states, **Rajasthan, Madhya Pradesh, Gujarat,** **and Maharashtra,** together account for **over 77.68%** of the national oilseed output. **So, statement IV is correct.**
● As of 2023–24, around **57% of the** **country’s edible oil demand is met through imports**, while the remaining demand is fulfilled by domestic oilseed crushing and processing. The **high import dependency** primarily arises from **limited** **domestic oilseed productivit**y and **rising consumption** demand. **So, statement II is correct.** **Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Agriculture
questions.questionText:
With reference to the millet production in India, consider the following statements:
I. - India’s share of global production is more than 50%.
II. 	 India exports less than 1% of its total millet production.
III. 	 Rajasthan is the largest producer of millets in India.
IV. 	 Jowar constitutes more than 50% of the total millet production.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Millets**, often termed **“nutri-cereals”,** are a group of small-seeded grains including **sorghum** (jowar), **pearl millet** (bajra), **finger millet** (ragi), **foxtail** millet, barnyard millet, proso millet, and kodo millet. They are rich in **protein, fiber, vitamins**, and essential minerals, and are both **gluten-free** and **low** **in glycemic index,** making them ideal for healthy and sustainable diets.
● As per FAO (2023), India accounts for around **38.4% of global millet production**, making it the largest producer but **not exceeding 50%** of the world’s share. While India leads globally, other milletproducing nations like **Nigeria, Niger, and China** also contribute significantly to global output. **So,** **statement I is not correct.**
● Despite being the world’s top producer, **India exports less than 1% of its total millet production.** For FY 2024–25, India’s total millet production was approximately 18.015 million tonnes, whereas exports were **only 0.089 million tonnes** (about 1.21 lakh metric tonnes).  **So, statement II is** **correct.**
● Among all Indian states, **Rajasthan** is the **largest producer** of millets. Other major producing states include **Uttar Pradesh, Karnataka, and Maharashtra**. Rajasthan’s arid **climate** and **sandy soils** **make** it ideally suited for millet cultivation.  **So, statement III is correct.**
● **Bajra** is also known as **pearl millet**, and its scientific name is Pennisetum typhoids. It is the **most** **cultivated** millet in India. In 2022-23, it contributed **66% to the total millet production in India.** **So, statement IV is not correct.** **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Geography
questions.questionText:
Consider the following species of trees:
I. - Cinchona
II. 	 Axlewood
III. 	 Mahua
IV. 	 Sal
V. 	 Rosewood
How many of the species given above belong to tropical evergreen forests?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Tropical Evergreen Forests** are found in **areas having more than 200 cm of rainfall and a** **mean annual temperature above 22 degrees C**. The tree here reaches great heights up to 60 metres or even above. Since the region is warm and wet throughout the year, it has a luxuriant vegetation of all kinds i.e. trees, shrubs and creepers, giving it a multilayered structure. There is no definite time for trees to shed their leaves.
● In India, this type of forest is found in heavy rainfall areas of the Western Ghats and the island groups of Andaman and Nicobar, the upper parts of Assam and the Tamil Nadu coast.
● Some of the commercially important trees of this forest are **ebony, mahogany, rosewood, rubber,** **aini, and cinchona**.
● **Cinchona** (Cinchona calisaya) is a **tropical evergreen tree**. The bark of the cinchona tree contains quinine, which is a medicine used to **treat malaria.** It is a flowering plant. It is native to the tropical Andean forests of western South America. **So, point I is correct.**
● **Axlewood** is a tree species of dry **Deciduous Forests.** It is a small to medium-sized, deciduous tree usually growing up to 20 metres tall. The tree is harvested for the large quantities of gum that flow from it. The tree also provides good-quality wood and charcoal. **So, point II is not correct.**
● **Sal is an important tree species of Deciduous Forests**. Sal Tree is indigenous to India. It occurs in two main regions, namely the northern and central Indian regions. **So, point IV is not correct.**
● **Mahua is a tree species of Deciduous Forests**. The Mahua is regarded as a medicinal plant with antibacterial, anticancer, hepatoprotective, antiulcer, antihyperglycemic, and analgesic properties. Mahua is known for its creamy-white flowers. These flowers are not only eaten raw or sun-dried as a nutritious food source but are also famously fermented to produce a strong, local brew. **So, point III is not** **correct.**
● **Rosewood** is a tree **species of tropical evergreen forests**. Rosewood is indigenous to India. It has hard, strong wood used for furniture, paneling, and ornamental works. It is distributed in the subHimalayan tract from eastern Uttar Pradesh to Sikkim. **So, point V is correct.** **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**
**Knowledge Box**
**Tropical Deciduous Forests:**
● These forests are found in regions that receive rainfall between 70-200 cm.
● These are the most widespread forests in India, and are also called the monsoon forests.
● The trees shed their leaves in the dry season.
● These forests are further divided into moist and dry deciduous.
● The **Moist deciduous forests** are more pronounced in the regions that record rainfall between 100-200 cm. These forests are found in the northeastern states along the foothills of the Himalayas, the eastern slopes of the Western Ghats and Odisha. **Teak,** **sal, shisham, hurra, mahua, amla, semul, kusum, and sandalwood** etc. are the main species of these forests.
● **Dry deciduous forests** are found in regions where rainfall ranges between 70 -100 cm. **Tendu, palas, amaltas, bel, khair, axlewood,** etc. are the common trees of these forests.

questions.subject: Geography
questions.questionText: Which of the following factors are responsible for the higher frequency of cyclones in the Bay of Bengal compared to the Arabian Sea?

options.a: Higher sea surface temperatures and greater moisture availability in the Bay of Bengal
options.b: Influx of typhoon remnants from the western Pacific Ocean that reintensify over the Bay of Bengal
options.c:
The trough-like geomorphological
configuration of the Bay of Bengal that aids cyclonic intensification

questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The **Bay of Benga**l witnesses a significantly **higher number of cyclones than the Arabian Sea** due to a combination of **thermal, atmospheric, and geographical factors.**
● Firstly, the Bay of Bengal maintains **higher sea surface temperatures** (around 29°C–30°C) throughout much of the year, providing the necessary **heat energy and humidity** for cyclone genesis. In contrast, the **Arabian Sea remains relatively cooler** owing to strong winds that enhance evaporation and the inflow of **dry air from desert regions** like Oman and Yemen, which inhibits storm development.
● Secondly, **remnants of typhoons** originating in the **western Pacific Ocean** often move westward **into the Bay of Bengal,** where they find favourable conditions to reintensify into cyclonic systems. This **external seeding effect** contributes to the higher cyclone frequency in the region.
● Thirdly, the **trough-like geomorphological structure** of the Bay of Bengal **facilitates air** **convergence and vertical motion**, enhancing the **intensification** process once a depression forms.
● **These combined conditions**—warm waters, abundant moisture, typhoon remnants, and conducive topography—**make the Bay of Bengal a more active cyclonic basin** compared to the Arabian Sea. **Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.**
**Relevance:** For every cyclone forming over the Arabian Sea, four cyclones develop over Bay of Bengal, according to Regional Specialised Meteorological Centre (RSMC) – Tropical Cyclones, India Meteorological Department (IMD).

questions.subject: Geography
questions.questionText:
With reference to the grasslands in India, consider the following statements:
I. - While tropical and sub-tropical grasslands are found in India, temperate grasslands are absent.
II. 	 Indian Wild Ass and Black Buck are found in tropical grasslands only.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● In India, grasslands account for approximately eight lakh sq. km, or about 24% of the country’s total land area.
● **Temperate grasslands are found in the Himalayas** at altitudes of 2,225-3048 m. They are found in cold areas of Jammu and Kashmir, Himachal, West Bengal and northeastern States. The grassland vegetation in the Indian Himalaya includes the **warm temperate grasslands, sub-alpine and cool** **temperate grassy slopes, alpine meadows of the greater Himalaya and the steppe** **formations of cold arid regions. So, statement I is not correct.**
● Tropical and sub-tropical grasslands are found in India. ○ For example, the Banni Grasslands is a tropical grassland located along the northern border of the Kachchh district in Gujarat. ○ The Kutch Desert Wildlife Sanctuary and Chhari Dhand Conservation Reserve are part of the Banni Grasslands. ○ In India, Savanna grasslands are found in the Terai-Duar region and semi-arid regions of peninsular India. The savannas of the Deccan plateau spread over Andhra Pradesh, Telangana, and Karnataka.
● **Indian Wild Ass and Black Buck** are considered the **flagship species** of grasslands in India. While both species are iconic representatives of Indian grasslands, they are **not confined exclusively to** **tropical grassland ecosystems**. Their distribution spans a wide range of habitats, including **saline** **deserts, scrublands, and dry deciduous forests** across different regions of India. **So, statement** **II is not correct.** **Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.**
**Point to Remember**
A flagship species is a charismatic animal or plant selected to serve as an ambassador, icon, or symbol for a particular environmental cause, such as the conservation of a threatened habitat or ecosystem. These species are chosen for their visual appeal, popularity, or cultural significance, as they help capture public interest and mobilize financial, political, and moral support for conservation efforts. For example: Bengal tiger, Giant Panda etc.

questions.subject: Geography
questions.questionText:
Consider the following pairs:
Volcano Result of
I. - Mt. Etna Convergence between the Eurasian Plate and the African Plate
II. - Mt. Cotopaxi
Convergence between the Cocos Plate and the South American Plate
III. Mt. Merapi Convergence between the Indo-Australian Plate and the Eurasian Plate
IV. Mt. Fuji Convergence between the Pacific Plate and the Eurasian Plate
How many of the above pairs are correctly matched?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● Mount Etna is located on the **island of** **Sicily, Italy**, near the city of **Catania**. It is one of the **most active volcanoes in the** **world** and a **UNESCO World Heritage** **Site**.
○ It is located above the **convergent** **plate** **margin** **between** **the** **African Plate and the Eurasian** **Plate.** Its activity is primarily driven by the subduction of the African Plate beneath the Eurasian Plate. **So, pair I is correctly** **matched.** ○ Mount Etna stands at around **3,300 metres**, about **four times the height of the Burj Khalifa**. ○ It is a **stratovolcano**, meaning it is **cone-shaped** and built up from **layers of viscous lava and** **ash**. ○ Approximately **60% of Earth’s volcanoes** are stratovolcanoes. ○ Etna exhibits both **effusive eruptions** (steady lava outpour) and **strombolian eruptions** (explosive bursts). ○ It remains in a **constant state of unrest**, unlike dormant volcanoes.
● **Mount Cotopaxi** is located in **Ecuador**, forming part of the **Andes Mountains** along the famous **“Avenue of** **Volcanoes.”**
○ It is a **stratovolcano**, created by the **subduction** **of the Nazca Plate beneath the South** **American Plate**, a classic case of **oceanic–** **continental convergence**. **So, pair II is not** **correctly matched.** ○ As the **denser Nazca Plate** sinks into the mantle, it **melts to form magma**, which rises to the surface, resulting in the formation of **volcanic** **chains** like Cotopaxi.
○ It is one of the **world s highest active volcanoes**.
● **Mount Merapi** lies in **Central Java, Indonesia**, above the **subduction zone** where the **Indo-Australian Plate** is forced beneath the **Eurasian Plate,** a classic case of **oceanic–continental convergence**.  **So, pair III is** **correctly matched.**
○ It is the **youngest** volcano in this alignment and one of the **most active stratovolcanoes in the world**.
● **Mount Fuji (Fujisan)** was formed at a **complex triple** **junction** involving the **Pacific Plate**, **Philippine Sea Plate**, and **Eurasian Plate**.  **So, pair IV is correctly matched.**
○ Both the **Pacific** and **Philippine Sea Plates** are **subducting beneath the Eurasian Plate**, creating intense volcanic activity. ○ This intricate **subduction process** led to the buildup of magma and the formation of Fuji as a **composite** **(stratovolcano)**. **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Geography
questions.questionText:
Which of the following statements is/are correct with regard to the Pamba River of South India?
I. - It originates from the  Pulachimalai Hill of the Western Ghats.
II. 	 It is the longest river in Kerala.
III.	 It is referred to as ‘Dakshina Bhageerathi’ due to its connection with Sabarimala.
IV. 	 Silt deposited by the river and its tributaries has created the fertile Kuttanad region, known as the “rice bowl of Kerala.”
Select the correct answer using the code given below:
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● Pamba River rises at an altitude of 1650 m **in the Pulachimalai Hill** on the Peerumedu plateau of the Western Ghats. **So, statement I is correct.**
● **Pamba is the third-longest river in Kerala** after the Periyar River and the Bharathappuzha River. It has a total length of 176 km. The entire catchment area lies in Kerala state. **So, statement II is not** **correct.**
● Pamba is a **west-flowing river that falls in the Vembanad lake**, which opens into the Arabian Sea. It does not drain directly into the Arabian Sea. So, Pamba **ultimately joins the Arabian Sea** through Vembanad Lake.
● The **Pamba is closely linked to the Sabarimala shrine**. It is crucial for the annual **Mandalam-** **Makaravilakku pilgrimage season** at the Sabarimala Lord Ayyappa temple. Pamba is now referred to as **‘Dakshina Bhageerathi’** due to its connection with Sabarimala.  Pilgrims visiting Sabarimala take a dip in the river here before and after trekking the hill. **So, statement III is correct.**
○ The main tributaries of the river are Pambayar, Kakki Ar, Arudai Ar, Kakkad Ar and Kallar.
● Silt deposited by Pamba and its tributaries has given Kerala the highly fertile land of **Kuttanad**, which is known as the **‘rice bowl of Kerala’.** It is one of the few places in the world where farming is done up to two metres below sea level. **So, statement IV is correct.** **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Geography
questions.questionText:
With regard to acidic soils  in India, consider the following statements:
I. - These are concentrated in regions with low rainfall and high evaporation rates, such as western Rajasthan.
II. 	 Carbonation pressmud is used for the reclamation of these soils.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Acid soils** develop in regions with **high rainfall and intense leaching of base nutrients**, such as the **North-Eastern states, Kerala, Odisha, and parts of the Himalayan region**. In contrast, areas with **low rainfall and high evaporation**, such as **western Rajasthan**, predominantly develop **arid** **and alkaline (sodic) soils**. **So, statement I is not correct.**
● **Pressmud**, a **residual byproduct** of sugar production also known as **filter cake** or **press cake**, has gained importance as a **resource for green energy generation**.
● Pressmud is **rich in many plant nutrients** and it also has properties to ameliorate degraded soils. Sulphitation pressmud can be used to amend the alkali soil, whereas carbonation pressmud is **useful to** **reclaim acidic soils. So, statement II is correct.**
**Points to Remember:**
● Indian soils exhibit a wide variation in pH, ranging from highly acidic to highly alkaline, but on average, a **significant portion of agricultural soils tends to be slightly** **alkaline**.
● Over **30% of cultivable land in India** contains **acidic soil**, which negatively affects **plant growth**. **Soils are becoming more acidic** due to **industrial activities** and **intense farming** practices.
● India has emerged as the **world’s foremost sugar producer** since **2021–22**, surpassing Brazil, and is also the **second-largest sugar exporter** globally.
**Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Geography
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements with regard to crude oil in India:
I. - India is the third-largest oil consumer in the world.
II. 	 Rajasthan is the largest onshore oil-producing State in India.
III. 	 India’s strategic petroleum reserves currently hold more than 50% of their total storage capacity.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Crude oil** is a **liquid petroleum** resource that accumulates in **porous rock formations** within the **Earth’s crust**.
● It is extracted for **burning as fuel** or processed into various **chemical products**. Chemically, crude oil is a **mixture of volatile liquid hydrocarbons**, mainly containing **hydrogen and carbon**, along with smaller amounts of **nitrogen, sulfur, and oxygen**.
● Despite variations in composition, most crude oils contain **82–87% carbon** and **12–15% hydrogen by** **weight**.
● India, the **world’s third-largest oil consumer and importer**, is projected to become the **leader in** **global oil demand growth** by **2030**, according to the **International Energy Agency (IEA)**. **So,** **statement I is correct.**
● India’s crude oil imports witnessed a significant shift in December 2024, with Iraq and Saudi Arabia being the top two suppliers of crude oil to India.
○ **Russia** has emerged as **India’s largest supplier of sunflower oil**, with imports increasing **twelvefold in the past four years**, replacing **Ukraine** as the top source.
● **Rajasthan is the largest onshore oil-producing state of India,** while the largest offshore producer of crude oil in India is the Mumbai High (formerly Bombay High) field, located in the Western Offshore region off the coast of Mumbai. **So, statement II is correct.**
● India’s **Strategic Petroleum Reserves (ISPR)** were established to **ensure energy security** during **global oil supply shocks**.  As of 2024, India’s **three strategic crude oil caverns** contain about **3.61** **million tonnes**, which is **67% of their rated capacity**, according to the **Lok Sabha Standing** **Committee on Petroleum and Natural Gas**. **So, statement III is correct.** **Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Geography
questions.questionText:
With reference to the Ganga River, which of the following statements is/are correct?
I. - The river system is an example of an antecedent drainage system.
II. 	 Its basin spreads over three countries only.
III. 	 Andha Galchi river gorge is carved by one of its tributaries.
Select the correct answer using the code given below:
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The Ganga River basin is the largest in India, encompassing **27%** of the country's land mass and supporting about **47%** of its population. Spanning over **11** **States**, the basin covers nearly **27%** **of** **India’s** **total** **geographical** **area**. The majority of the basin, around
**65.57%,** is used for agriculture, while water bodies cover **3.47%** of the area. Despite receiving
**35.5%** of the total water input in terms of precipitation, the Ganga River Basin is the second most water-stressed basin in India, following the Sabarmati Basin, with only **39%** of the average per capita annual rainwater input among major Indian river basins.
● It **rises in the Gangotri glacier** near Gaumukh (3,900 m) in the Uttarkashi district of Uttarakhand. ○ At its source, the river is called the Bhagirathi. It descends down the valley upto Devprayag, where after joining another hill stream, Alaknanda, it is called Ganga.
● The **Ganga River system** is a classic examples of **antecedent drainage systems**. **So, statement I** **is correct.**
○ An antecedent river is one that existed prior to the uplift of the land (for example, the Himalayas) and was able to maintain its course by eroding its bed at approximately the same rate as the land was uplifted, thereby cutting deep gorges through the rising mountains.
● The **Ganga basin is spread in India, Tibet (China), Nepal and Bangladesh** over an area of 10,86,000 sq km. Basin is the area drained by a river and its tributaries. **So, statement II is not** **correct.**
○ The Ganga joins the Brahmaputra in Bangladesh and continues its run under the name Padma or Ganga. It finally joins the Meghna river, which outfalls into the Bay of Bengal.
● The important **left bank tributaries** are the **Ramganga, the Gomati, the Ghaghara, the Gandak,** **the Kosi and the Mahananda**. The **Yamuna, Son and Damodar** are its major **right bank** **tributaries.** The **Yamuna is the westernmost and the longest tributary of the Ganga**. The river finally discharges itself into the Bay of Bengal near Sagar Island.
● The **Gandak River**, known as the **Kali Gandaki in its upper reaches in Nepal**, has carved the **Andha Galchi (Kali Gandaki Gorge)** between the **Dhaulagiri and Annapurna** mountain peaks. By some measurements, which compare the riverbed with the surrounding mountain peaks, this gorge is regarded as **one of the deepest in the world**. **So, statement III is correct.** **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Geography
questions.questionText:
With reference to Chillai Kalan, consider the following statements:
I. - It is a 40-day harsh winter period experienced in the upper regions of Himachal Pradesh.
II. 	 It provides favourable conditions for the growth of apples.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Chillai Kalan** is the **harshest winter period in the Kashmir Valley**, lasting 40 days from December 21 to January 29 every year. The term is derived from Persian, where ‘**Chilla’ means “to confine** **indoors”** and **‘Kalan’ means “major” or “intense.”** This period marks the peak of winter, characterized by subzero temperatures, **heavy snowfall, frozen lakes** (including the Dal Lake), and limited outdoor activity. It plays an important role in the region’s **agro-climatic cycle and cultural** **heritage. So, statement I is not correct.**
● Despite the hardships caused by **freezing temperatures** and blocked roads, the freezing of water bodies and prolonged cold during Chillai Kalan are **beneficial for the apple-growing segment of** **Kashmir.** The cold ensures proper chilling hours for apple trees, which is **essential for bud** **formation** and subsequent flowering in spring. **So, statement II is correct.**
○ Chilling requirements (CR) refer to the specific period of cold temperatures that perennial tree crops, such as apples and cherries, must experience to break winter dormancy and resume growth in the spring. Failure to meet these requirements can lead to delayed foliation, irregular flowering, and variable fruit quality and maturity at harvest. **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**
**Relevance:** One of the harshest winter periods, called Chillai Kalan, has begun in Kashmir.

questions.subject: Geography
questions.questionText:
Consider the following pairs:
River Forms a Boundary Between India and
I. - Chenab Pakistan
II. - Sharda Nepal
III. - Chhimtuipui Myanmar
IV. - Kushiyara China
Which of the pairs given above are correctly matched?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **The River Chenab is** an **important tributary of** **the Indus**. It **originates from the Lahaul valley** of Himachal Pradesh.  The river Chenab (or Chandra Bhaga) is formed after the two streams, the Chandra and the Bhaga, merge with each other. In India, the watershed of the **Chenab basin covers part of** **two States viz. Himachal Pradesh and Jammu** **and Kashmir.** It finally meets the Satluj in Pakistan to form the Panchnad, which joins the Indus River at Mithankot. **It does not form a boundary between India and Pakistan.**
○ **Ravi, Satluj and Jhelum** form the **India-Pakistan boundary.** **So, pair I is not correctly** **matched.**
● **Sharda or Kali forms the natural border between India and Nepal** in the Uttarakhand region. It is known as Kali in the upper reaches and Mahakali in Nepal. **The Sharda River** rises in the **Milam** **glacier in Uttarakhand**. **Along the Indo-Nepal** **border, it is called Kali** or Chauk. It eventually joins the Ghaghara River (tributary of the Ganga River). Key tributaries of the Sharda River are the Dhauliganga, the Goriganga, and the Sarju River. **So, pair II is correctly** **matched.**
● The **Kaladan River is called the Chhimtuipui River** in India. It **forms the international border between** **India and Myanmar**. River Kaladan (Chhimtuipui) emanates from central Mizoram and flows into the Bay of Bengal at Sittwe in Myanmar. It is Mizoram's largest river. The river meanders along the Indo-Myanmar Border in Mizoram and finally empties itself at Sittwe. **So, pair III is correctly matched.**
● **Kushiyara is a transboundary river between** **India and Bangladesh.** Kushiyara is a distributary of the Barak River, which flows through Assam and then on to Bangladesh. The Barak River, originating in India, bifurcates at the India-Bangladesh border at Amalshid into two rivers, viz. Surma and Kushiyara. The two rivers flow along the border of the two countries before finally entering Bangladesh**. So, pair IV is not** **correctly matched.** **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Geography
questions.questionText:
With reference to the temperature gradient,
consider the following statements:
I. - Narrow spacing between isotherms indicates
a high thermal gradient.
II. 	 It is generally low over the tropical regions.
III. 	 It is generally high over the eastern margins
of continents.
Which of the statements given above are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The **temperature gradient** refers to the **rate of change of temperature** over a given distance. It is represented on maps using **isotherms** (lines connecting places of equal temperature). The pattern and **spacing of isotherms** help identify how rapidly or slowly **temperature changes** occur from one region to another. Temperature gradients are influenced by **latitude, land-ocean contrast, ocean** **currents, and altitude.**
● When **isotherms are closely spaced,** the temperature difference between adjacent locations is large over a short distance. This indicates a **high temperature gradient.** Conversely, **widely spaced** isotherms represent a **low temperature gradient.** Thus, narrow spacing corresponds to rapid temperature change. **So, statement I is correct.**
● **Over the tropics,** the **sun** is nearly **overhead** throughout the year, resulting in **relatively uniform** **heating.** Because temperatures do not vary sharply with distance, **temperature gradients** in the tropics are **generally low.** This is why isotherms in the tropical belt are spaced more widely. **So,** **statement II is correct.**
● The **eastern margins of continents** are usually influenced by **warm ocean currents,** which have a moderating effect, leading to **more uniform temperatures** and therefore **low temperature** **gradients.** In contrast, **western margins** of continents are influenced by cold ocean currents, which **intensify temperature contrasts;** hence, temperature gradients are high on western margins, not eastern ones. **So, statement III is not correct.** **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Agriculture
questions.questionText:
With reference to the cultivation of Bamboo in
India, consider the following statements:
I. - It usually grows in warm and moist climates.
II. 	 India is the second-largest producer of
bamboo in the world.
III. 	 The North-Eastern region accounts for more
than one-fourth of India’s total bamboo area.
Which of the statements given above is/are
correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● Bamboo is an **evergreen, perennial, and flowering plant** that holds significant ecological and economic importance. In India, it is widely cultivated as a **commercial crop** and is often referred to as the **“poor man’s timber”** due to its affordability, versatility, and extensive use in construction, furniture making, and handicrafts. Among the various regions suitable for bamboo cultivation, the **Barak** **Valley** in northeastern India offers particularly favorable conditions. The region’s **fertile soil, high** **humidity, and moderate temperature** create an ideal environment for bamboo growth, making it one of the most productive areas for bamboo cultivation in the country.
● Bamboo is mostly found in tropical and sub-tropical regions. It also grows in mild temperate regions. Bamboo plantations grow well in tropical to warm temperate climatic conditions. A combined effect of high temperature and rainfall promotes the growth. **Most bamboo species are native to warm and** **moist tropical and to warm temperate climates. So, statement I is correct.**
● **India ranks 2nd in the production of bamboo worldwide.** India has 13.96 million hectares of bamboo cultivation**. So, statement II is correct.**
● **The North-Eastern region** accounts for a significant share of India’s bamboo resources, comprising about **28% of the country’s total bamboo area and over 50% of its bamboo species diversity.** **So, statement III is correct.** **Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.**
**Knowledge Box**
**About Bamboo:**
● Importance of Bamboo: ○ Bamboo shoots of some species are edible and have high nutritional value. ○ The plant improves the environment in many significant ways including acting as **an atmospheric and soil purifier.** ○ It is hardy, light and flexible, thus a good substitute for wood. ○ Bamboo is extensively used in paper and rayon-textile manufacturing, construction, architecture, engineering, handicrafts, food, and medicine. ○ Environmentally, bamboo **helps mitigate land degradation, sequester** **carbon, and combat climate change.**

questions.subject: Geography
questions.questionText:
Which one of the following best defines a
'fumarole' in geological terms?

options.a: A deep-sea trench formed due to subduction
options.b:
An opening in the Earth’s crust emitting
steam and volcanic gases
options.c: A mass of ice detached from a glacier
options.d:
A type of fertile soil found in the Himalayan
foothills

questions.explanationMarkdown:
● A **fumarole** is an **opening in the Earth’s crust** that emits **steam** **and volcanic gases** such as **sulfur dioxide, hydrogen sulfide, and** **carbon dioxide**. They are commonly found in **active volcanic** **regions**, along **cracks**, and on **lava surfaces**.
● Fumaroles form when **hot magma** beneath the surface heats **groundwater**, creating **steam and gas pressure** that escapes through cracks or vents.
● These are formed **when an active volcano stops erupting lava** and moves towards a dormant state.
● Depending on gas composition, a **CO**₂**-rich fumarole** is called a **mofette**, and a **sulfur-rich** one is called a **solfatara**.
● They typically emit gases at **70°C–100°C or higher**.
● Fumaroles are indicators of **ongoing volcanic activity**, appearing after eruptions when **magma** **pressure decreases** and **gases escape** through vents.
● The gases are **toxic to humans**, so areas near fumaroles are **unsafe for habitation**.
● In the case of volcanic eruptions, the main vent directly aligned with deposits of hot magma will discharge magma. Upon reaching the surface of the Earth, magma cools as it flows. When pressure from within the Earth is extremely high, the sides of volcanic mountains may break away as magma is discharged through side vents.
● The flow of magma continues until the underground pressure is greatly reduced, and gas fumes and hot vapor are eventually emitted from fumaroles. However, **fumaroles do not emit lava.** **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Geography
questions.microTopic: Critical and Strategic Minerals (Lithium, Cobalt, Titanium, KABIL, REEs, etc.)
questions.questionText:
With reference to silver and its production in India, consider the following statements:
I. - Argentite is an important ore of silver.
II. 	 The Zawar mines in Rajasthan are a major source of silver in India.
III. 	 India is self-sufficient in silver production.
Which of the statements given above are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● Silver is mainly found in **lead, zinc, gold, and copper ore** **deposits**, and its **most important ore mineral** is **argentite (Ag**₂**S)**. The extraction of silver from ore is usually carried out through **smelting** or **chemical** **leaching**. **So, statement I is correct.**
● Silver is **rarely found in native form**, and instead commonly occurs combined with elements like **sulfur,** **arsenic, antimony, or chlorine**, forming minerals such as **argentite**, **chlorargyrite**, and **galena**.
● The **Zawar mines** in the **Udaipur district of Rajasthan** are a major source of silver in India. Silver is obtained there mainly as a **by-product of lead–zinc (galena) ore** **processing**. **So, statement II is correct.**
● India is **not self-sufficient in silver production**. Although domestic silver is produced as a by-product of base metal mining, the country depends heavily on **imports** to meet its substantial **industrial,** **jewellery, and cultural demand**. **So, statement III is not correct.** **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**
**Relevance:** Mines and Minerals (Development and Regulation) Amendment Bill provides that leaseholders may apply to the state government for adding other minerals (lithium, graphite, nickel, cobalt, gold, and silver) to an existing lease.

questions.subject: Geography
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements:
I. - Its basin covers nearly 4.3% of the total geographical area of India.
II. 	 It is bounded by the Central Indian Hills on the north, by the Eastern Ghats on the south and east, and by the Maikala range on the west.
III. 	 The major portion of its drainage basin lies in Chhattisgarh.
IV. 	 Its meandering course has led to the formation of Ansupa Lake.
Which one of the following rivers is described above?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **The Mahanadi River** is often described as the lifeline of Odisha. It originates from **Sihawa Mountain** in Chhattisgarh and flows through the states of Chhattisgarh and Odisha.
○ It is **bounded** by the **Central Indian Hills** on the north, by the **Eastern Ghats on the** south and east, and by the **Maikala range** on the west.
● The **Mahanadi basin** covers an area of about 1.42 lakh sq km, accounting for **nearly 4.3% of India’s** **total geographical area.**
● The Mahanadi River flows through the states of **Chhattisgarh and Odisha, with the majority of its** **drainage basin lying in Chhattisgarh.** The total length of the river from its origin to its outfall into the Bay of Bengal is 851 km. The Seonath, the Hasdeo, the Mand, and the Ib join the Mahanadi from left, whereas the Ong, the Tel, and the Jonk join it from right.
● **Chilka Lake** is **fed by its two branches,** namely **Daya and Bhargavi.** ○ Chilka is the **largest brackish water lake in India**. It is located along the eastern coast of India in Odisha. It is a wetland of international importance under the Ramsar Convention.
● **Ansupa Lake** is a horseshoe-shaped water body. It is **Odisha's largest natural freshwater lake**. It is located on the banks of the Mahanadi River near Banki in Cuttack district. **The lake is an ox-bow** **lake of the Mahanadi River.** **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Geography
questions.microTopic: Physiography of India (Location, Division, Physiographic Regions)
questions.questionText:
Consider the following hills:
I. - Mahadeo
II. 	 Garhjat
III. 	 Satmala
IV. 	 Gawilgarh
How many of the hills given above are part of the Satpura Range?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The **Peninsular Plateau** is an ancient tabular block composed mostly of the archean gneisses and schist. It is essentially an aggregation of several smaller plateaus and hill ranges interspersed with river basins and valleys.
● **Some prominent hill ranges of the Peninsular Plateau are:** ○ The **Mahadeo Hills** are located in **Madhya Pradesh**. It forms a **part of the Satpura Range.** **So, point I is correct.** ○ The **Garhjat Hills** is a mountain in **Eastern India**. It stretches mainly across the State of **Odisha. So, point II is not correct.** ○ The **Satmala Hills** are located in the Nashik District of **Maharashtra**. They are a **part of the** **Sahyadri range. So, point III is not correct.**
○ **Gawilgarh Hills** are a part of the **Satpura Range of Madhya Pradesh**. It is located between the Rajpipla Hills in the West and the Mahadeo Hills to the East. **So, point IV is correct.**
**So, only two of four are part of the Satpura Range.** **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Geography
questions.microTopic: Critical and Strategic Minerals (Lithium, Cobalt, Titanium, KABIL, REEs, etc.)
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements with regard to Rare Earth Elements (REEs) in India:
I. - India ranks among the top five countries in the world in terms of rare earth deposits.
II. 	  More than 90% of India’s rare earth imports by value and quantity were sourced from China in 2024–25..
III. 	 Monazite is the principal source of rare earths in India.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Rare Earth Elements (REEs) constitute a group of 17 chemically similar elements,** **comprising the 15 lanthanides along with scandium and yttrium.** Although these elements are relatively abundant in the Earth’s crust, they are termed “rare” because they typically occur in low concentrations and are dispersed within complex mineral matrices. This makes their extraction and separation technologically challenging and economically costly.
● Based on **atomic number**, REEs are classified into **light REEs (Cerium group)** and **heavy REEs** **(Yttrium group)**.
● India has the **third-largest reserves** **(**8 per cent of the world’s Rare Earth Element (REE) Reserves**) of** **Rare Earth Elements** with **6.9 million metric tonnes**, mainly located in **Andhra Pradesh,** **Karnataka, Odisha and Kerala**, especially in **monazite sands**, which are India’s **principal source** **of REEs**. **So, statements I is correct and III are correct.**
● As per the **Indian Mineral Yearbook (2023)**, India’s estimated **monazite resources** stand at **12.73** **million tonnes**, with **Andhra Pradesh** having the highest reserves at **3.78 million tonnes**.
● **India produces less than 1%** of the **global REE output**. Most available REEs in India are **light REEs** such as **Lanthanum, Cerium and Samarium**, whereas **heavy REEs like Dysprosium and** **Terbium** face **supply constraints**, and **China dominates** the global heavy REE production and supply chain.
● India sourced **93% of its Rare Earth Elements (REEs)** from **China** in **2024–25. So, statement II** **is correct.**. **Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Geography
questions.questionText: The terms ‘Norte, Norther, and Berg’ refer to:

options.a: Hot and dry tropical winds of the Sahara
options.b: Warm downslope winds occurring in Alpine regions
options.c: Cold local winds blowing in different parts of the world
options.d: Seasonal monsoon reversal winds in the South China region

questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Norte, Norther,** **and Berg** are **cold** **local winds** that occur in different regions due to the southward or **downslope** **movement** **of** **cold continental** **air masses.**
● **Norte** is a cold wind that blows from the North American continent toward the **Gulf of Mexico** and eastern Mexico, often causing a **sharp fall in temperature**, strong gales, and rough seas.
● **Norther** is a similar **cold outbreak** affecting the **Great Plains of the United States,** moving southward from **Canada**, producing **sudden cold waves, frost,** and severe winter conditions.
● **Berg wind** (from the Afrikaans word "berg" meaning mountain) occurs in **South Africa,** blowing from the interior plateau **toward the coast.** It is initially warm and dry while **descending the escarpment** but is associated with the arrival of cold maritime air that follows, leading to a **rapid temperature** **drop** and unsettled weather conditions along the coast. **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**
**Knowledge Box**
**Warm Wind** **Cold Wind**
Chinook (USA) Gregale (western and central Mediterranean region)
Fohn (Switzerland) Mistral (southern France)
Zonda (Argentina) Bora (Shores of Adriatic Sea)
Sirocco (Sahara to Mediterranean) Pampero (South America)

questions.subject: Geography
questions.questionText:
In which of the following ways do the Himalayas influence the climate system of India?
I. - They block the cold winds from Central Asia, preventing extreme cold conditions in northern India.
II. 	 They obstruct the southwest monsoon winds, resulting in heavy rainfall in the Indian subcontinent.
III. 	 They create convection currents, contributing to snowfall in the northernmost parts of India.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The **Himalayas,** stretching across northern India, are not only the **world’s youngest and highest** **mountain range** but also a **major climatic divide** that shapes the subcontinent’s weather and seasonal patterns.
● The Himalayas act as a **natural barrier** that **blocks cold continental winds from Central Asia,** **not from Antarctica.** These Central Asian winds, if unobstructed, would drastically lower **winter** **temperatures** in northern India. By deflecting them, the Himalayas help maintain a relatively moderate **winter climate across northern India,** especially the Indo-Gangetic plains. **So, statement I is** **correct.**
● During the **southwest monsoon season** (June–September), the Himalayas **obstruct the moist** **monsoon winds** blowing from the Arabian Sea and Bay of Bengal. As these air masses strike the mountain slopes, they are forced to rise, leading to **adiabatic cooling** and condensation. This process causes **orographic rainfall,** especially along the southern Himalayan foothills and the northeastern states. Thus, the Himalayas are instrumental in producing the **heavy monsoonal rainfall** that sustains India’s agriculture and river systems. **So, statement II is correct.**
● While the Himalayas influence wind flow and precipitation, **they do not create convection currents.** Convection currents arise primarily due to **temperature differentials** between the Earth’s surface and the atmosphere, leading to vertical air movement. The **snowfall** in the Himalayan region is mainly **due to orographic uplift and western disturbances**, not convection. **So, statement III is not** **correct.** **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Geography
questions.microTopic: Demography, Population, Census (Literacy, Sex Ratio, etc.)
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements with regard to the demography of India:
I. - India entered its demographic dividend window in 2005-2006 and is expected to remain in it until 2055–56.
II. 	 About 65% of India’s population is under 35 years of age, with a median age of around 28 years.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **India has become the world’s most** **populous country**, overtaking China, with **UN** **data** showing India at 1.42 billion and China at
1.42 billion.
● **Demographic** **dividend** refers to the **economic growth potential** arising when the **working-age population (15–64 years)** becomes larger than the dependents.
● India entered this **demographic window in** **2005–06** and will remain in it till **2055–56**. **So,** **statement I is correct.**
● The **Economic Survey 2018–19** notes that the dividend will **peak around 2041** when the **20–59 age group reaches 59%** of the population.
● With over **65% of its population under 35 and a median age of 28** compared to 38 in China and 48 in Japan, India possesses a dynamic labour force capable of driving productivity. **So, statement II is** **correct.** **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**
**Knowledge Box**
● India retains a **significant young population**, with **24% aged 0–14**, **17% aged 10–** **19**, and **26% aged 10–24**.
● The **elderly population (65 years and older) stands at 7%, a share expected** **to rise steadily as life expectancy has improved to 71 years for men and 74** **years for women by** 2025.
● Around **68% of the population** is in the **working-** **age group (15–64 years)**, strengthening India’s demographic advantage.
● According to the **United Nations Population** **Fund (UNFPA)**, India is set to remain one of the youngest countries until 2055, offering a multi-decade window to leverage its youthful workforce.
● According to the UN report **“State of the World** **Population 2025: The Real Fertility Crisis.”** India’s **Total Fertility Rate (TFR)** has fallen to **1.9**, which is **below the** **replacement level** of **2.1**. India is projected to become **170 crore** before its population **starts declining in around 40 years**.

questions.subject: Geography
questions.questionText:
Consider the following pairs:
Landform Geomorphic Agent
I. - Skerry Ground water
II. - Kettle hole Glacier
III. - Zeugen Wind
IV. - Mushroom rock Running water
How many of the pairs given above are correctly matched?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● Landforms on the earth’s surface are created by various **geomorphic agents** such as wind, rivers, glaciers, groundwater and sea waves. These agents shape the terrain through **erosion, deposition and** **weathering**, forming distinct physical features.
● **Stacks and skerries are marine erosional landforms formed through the progressive action** **of wave erosion on coastal headlands.** When the roof of a sea arch collapses due to continued erosion and gravitational forces, an isolated, steep-sided column of rock remains offshore. This remnant structure is referred to as a skerries. **So, pair I is not correctly matched.**
● **Kettle holes** (or **kettle lakes**) are **depressions formed by glaciers**. When large blocks of glacial ice get detached and buried under sediments, they eventually melt, creating **hollow depressions or** **small lakes**. These are found in **glacial outwash plains. So, pair II is correctly matched.**
● **Zeugen** are **tabular landforms** consisting of **soft rock layers overlain by harder rock**. Due to the erosional action of **wind (aeolian erosion)**, the softer rock beneath is gradually worn away, while the harder upper layer remains intact, forming **flat-topped, steep-sided ridges**. These ridges are characteristic features of **desert landscapes**, with notable examples found in the **Sahara Desert**. **So,** **pair III is correctly matched.**
● **Mushroom rocks**, also known as **pedestal rocks**, are formed primarily due to **wind erosion**, not river action. Wind carrying sand particles erodes the lower part of the rock more rapidly than the top, because wind-blown sand is most effective near the ground. This results in a **narrow base and a wider** **top**, giving the rock its mushroom shape. **So, pair IV is not correctly matched.** **So, only two of the pairs given above are correctly matched.** **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer**.
**Knowledge Box**
● **Other erosional marine landforms:** ○ **Chasms (notches):** narrow, deep indentations formed through headward erosion of weaknesses in rock. ○ **Wave-cut platforms:** horizontal or gently sloping benches formed when cliffs retreat under wave attack. ○ **Blow-holes or spouting horns:** features where compressed air/water is forced out of small rock openings, causing bursts or sprays. ○ **Peneplain:** when marine erosion levels the coast to a plain; weathering may further reduce relief.
● **Marine depositional landforms:** ○ **Beaches:** accumulation of sand/gravel/rock debris along the shore or on the wave-cut platform. ○ **Bars:** submerged or partly emergent ridges of sand or rock debris built by currents/tides parallel to the shore. ○ **Spits and hooks:** depositional landforms projecting from the shore into the sea (spit); when curved landward, it forms a hook. ○ **Tombolos:** bars or ridges that connect an island to the mainland or another island.
● **Glacial erosional landforms:** ○ **Cirque (Corrie):** Bowl-shaped hollow at the glacier’s head, often containing a small lake (tarn) after melting. ○ **Arete:** Sharp, narrow ridge between two cirques or glacial valleys. ○ **Horn:** Pyramidal peak formed by the erosion of several cirques around a mountain top. ○ **Fjord:** Drowned U-shaped valley filled with seawater after glaciatio**n.**
● **Glacial depositional landforms** ○ **Moraine:** Accumulation of unsorted glacial debris (till); includes lateral, medial, terminal, and ground types. ○ **Outwash Plain:** Flat area of stratified sand and gravel deposited by glacial meltwater beyond the ice front. ○ **Esker:** Long, winding ridge of sand and gravel deposited by subglacial meltwater streams. ○ **Drumlin:** Smooth, elongated hill of till aligned with the direction of ice movement.
● **Wind eroded landforms:** ○ **Deflation Hollow (Blow-out):** A shallow depression created by wind removal of loose surface material. ○ **Inselberg:** An isolated rocky hill or dome rising abruptly from an otherwise flat desert plain.
○ **Yardang:** A streamlined ridge sculpted by wind abrasion and aligned with the prevailing wind. ○ **Demoiselle:** A slender rock column left standing as softer material around it is eroded away. ○ **Zeugen:** A flat-topped ridge of hard rock protecting softer layers beneath from wind erosion.
● **Wind depositional landforms:** ○ **Longitudinal Dune:** A dune ridge running parallel to the direction of prevailing winds. ○ **Transverse Dune:** A dune ridge oriented perpendicular to the main wind direction. ○ **Barchan Dune:** A crescent-shaped dune with tips pointing downwind. ○ **Parabolic Dune:** A U-shaped dune whose arms face upwind, often anchored by vegetation. ○ **Star Dune:** A dune with multiple arms radiating from a central peak, shaped by variable winds. ○ **Loess:** Fine, wind-deposited silt or dust forming fertile but easily erodible soils.

questions.subject: Geography
questions.questionText:
With reference to Hot Spot Volcanism, consider the following statements:
I. - It occurs primarily at the convergent plate boundary.
II. 	 The Deccan Traps in India were formed as a result of hot-spot volcanic activity.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● Hot spot volcanism refers to **volcanic activity that occurs away from plate boundaries**, caused by **plumes of abnormally hot magma (mantle plumes)** rising from deep within the Earth’s mantle. Unlike normal volcanism associated with **plate margins** (divergent or convergent boundaries), hotspot volcanism takes place in the **interior of tectonic plates**, forming island chains, plateaus, and volcanic provinces.  **So, statement I is not correct.**
● The **Deccan Traps**, one of the world’s largest volcanic provinces, were formed by volcanic activity triggered by the Indian Plate moving over a stationary hotspot, specifically the Réunion hotspot**. So,** **statement II is correct.** **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**
**Knowledge Box**
Both **Iceland** and **St. Helena** are examples of volcanic islands formed due to **hot spot** **activity**. Iceland lies on the **Mid-Atlantic Ridge**, where a mantle plume coincides with it, leading to extensive volcanic activity. **St. Helena**, located in the South Atlantic Ocean, is also of volcanic origin, formed by an **intraplate hot spot**.

questions.subject: Geography
questions.questionText:
With reference to the Andes Mountains, consider the following statements:
I. - They are fold mountains formed due to the divergence between the Nazca Plate and the South American Plate.
II. 	 They host the world’s highest active volcano, namely Ojos del Salado.
III. 	 Mount McKenzie is the highest peak in both the Andes mountain range and South America.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The **Andes Mountains** are the **longest continental mountain ranges in the world**, along the western coast of South America. It consists of a succession of parallel and transverse mountain ranges, or cordilleras, and of intervening plateaus and depressions.
● The Andes Mountains were formed due to **convergence (not divergence)** between the **Nazca Plate** **and the South American Plate**. The **oceanic Nazca Plate subducts beneath the continental** **South American Plate**, leading to fold mountain formation. Divergent boundaries, on the other hand, create mid-ocean ridges or rift valleys. **So, statement I is not correct.**
● **Ojos del Salado**, located on the **Chile–Argentina border**, is the **world’s highest active volcano**. It is located in Andes mountain range. It remains a significant site for studying **volcanic and tectonic** **activity** associated with subduction zones. **So, statement II is correct.**
● The **highest peak of the Andes** is **Aconcagua**, located in **Argentina**. It is also the **highest peak in** **South America.** **So, statement III is not correct.** **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**
**Knowledge Box**
**Fold mountains** arise due to **horizontal compression of the Earth’s crust**, which bends and folds sedimentary rock layers into wave-like structures. They are typically located along convergent plate boundaries where two plates collide.
● **Himalayas (India):** Formed by the collision of the Indian and Eurasian plates; still rising at ~5 mm per year (Geological Survey of India, 2022).
● **Alps (Europe):** Created by the convergence of the African and Eurasian plates.
● **The Andes** (South America) and the **Rockies** (North America) are also major fold mountain systems.

questions.subject: Geography
questions.microTopic: Physiography of India (Location, Division, Physiographic Regions)
questions.questionText:
Consider the following hills/ranges of the Himalayas:
I. - Manaslu
II. 	 Indrasan
III. 	 Kedarnath
IV. 	 Nanda Devi
V. 	 Nag Tibba
How many of the above form a part of the Lesser Himalayas, also known as the Himachal Himalaya?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The Himalayan range is a young fold mountain. It runs in a west-east direction and comprises a chain of parallel mountain ranges. The **three** **main ranges** are, the  **Greater Himalayas** **or Himadri, the Lesser Himalayas or** **Himachal, and the Shiwalik**.
● The Greater Himalayas are the northernmost range, known as the **Himadri**. It is the most continuous range. It contains all the prominent Himalayan peaks. Important peaks in this range are **Mt. Everest, Kanchenjunga, Makalu, Dhaulagiri, Manaslu, and Nanda Devi.**
○ **Manaslu is a part of the Greater Himalayas.** Manaslu in Nepal, is the 8th highest mountain in the world. **So, point I is not correct.** ○ **Mount Kedarnath is located in the Greater Himalayas.** Kedarnath (Kedarnath Main) and Kedarnath Dome (Kedar Dome) are two mountains in the Gangotri Group of peaks in western Garhwal Himalaya in Uttarakhand. The temple of Kedarnath is set against the Kedarnath peak. **So, point III is not correct.**
○ **Nanda Devi is a part of the Greater Himalayas.** It is part of the Garhwal Himalayas, and is located in the state of Uttarakhand. It is India’s second highest mountain. **So, point IV is not** **correct.**
● **Lesser Himalayas** or **Himachal** lies to the south of the Greater Himalayas. The ranges are mainly composed of highly compressed and altered rocks.
○ The **Pir Panjal, Dhauladhar, Nag Tibba and Mahabharat** are the ranges of the Lesser Himalaya. ○ **Indrasan is the highest peak of the Pir Panjal range** of lesser Himalayas. It is located in the Kullu district of Himachal Pradesh. **So, point II is correct.** ○ **Nag Tibba, also known as the Serpent's Peak, is the highest peak in the Nag Tibba** **range of the Garhwal Himalayas.**It is in Garhwal Himalayas of Uttarakhand. **So, point V is** **correct.**
● The Outer Himalayas or the Shiwaliks is the outermost range of the Himalayas. **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Geography
questions.questionText:
Consider the following pairs:
Lake Fed by the River
I. - Wular Chenab
II. - Pulicat Krishna
III. - Vembanad Periyar
IV. - Sambhar Luni
How many of the pairs given above are correctly matched?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Wular Lake in Jammu and Kashmir is formed by the** **meandering of the Jhelum River**, an important tributary of the Indus. It is the second largest freshwater lake of Asia and **the** **largest in India.**  **So, pair I is not correctly matched.**
● **Pulicat is the second largest brackish water lagoon in** **India.** It lies partly in Tamil Nadu and partly in Andhra Pradesh State. It is **fed by three rivers – the Arani, the** **Swarnamukhi and the Kalangi**. **So, pair II is not correctly** **matched.**
● The **Vembanad lake** is fed by many rivers flowing into it including the Achenkovil, Manimala, Meenachil, Muvattupuzha, **Pamba and Periyar.**is India’s longest lake and a Ramsar site. **So, pair III is correctly matched.**
● **Sambhar Lake is India's largest saline wetland.** It is also a designated Ramsar Site (recognized wetland of international importance), attracting migratory birds from northern Asia and Siberia from November to February. **It is fed mainly by the Mendha and** **Rupangarh rivers. So, pair IV is not correctly matched.**
○ **Luni** is the most significant river basin of Western Rajasthan. Luni **originates from the western slopes of the Aravalli** **ranges** in Ajmer district and finally flows into the Rann of Kachchh.
**So, only one  of the pairs given above is correctly matched.** **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Geography
questions.questionText:
In the context of earthquakes, consider the following statements:
I. - Love waves are the most destructive body waves.
II. 	 Rayleigh waves produce a rolling ground motion and cause major earthquake damage.
III. 	 Reverse faults at convergent boundaries produce megathrust earthquakes.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● During an earthquake, the **energy released at** **the focus** travels outward in the form of **seismic waves**. These waves move through the Earth’s interior and along its surface, causing the Earth to shake.
● There are **three main types of seismic** **waves**: **Primary (P) waves**, **Secondary (S)** **waves**, and **Surface waves**.
● Surface waves travel **along the Earth’s** **surface** and usually cause the **most damage** during an earthquake. They are slower than both P and S waves and include two main types: **Love waves** and **Rayleigh waves**.
○ **Love waves** involve **horizontal transverse motion**, perpendicular to the direction of propagation and parallel to the Earth’s surface. They travel at speeds of **2.0–4.5 km/s** depending on frequency. Love waves cause strong **side-to-side shaking** and a decrease in amplitude with depth. ○ Love waves are **surface waves, not body waves**, and they are among the **most destructive** **seismic waves**. They cause horizontal shearing, which can be devastating to structures, especially at the epicenter. **So, statement I is not correct.** ○ **Rayleigh waves** move in an **elliptical path**, both **vertical and horizontal**, similar to the motion of water waves.
■ Their velocity is also around **2.0–4.5 km/s**, and their amplitude decreases with depth.
■ Rayleigh waves **produce** a **rolling motion** on the ground surface and are a major cause
of **structural damage** during earthquakes. **So, statement II is correct.**
● **Reverse faults at convergent boundaries** are responsible for producing some of the most **powerful** **earthquakes on Earth,** often referred to as **megathrust** earthquakes. These occur where one tectonic plate is forced beneath another (subduction zones), and nearly all earthquakes of magnitude 8 or higher are generated. **So, statement III is correct.** **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**
**Knowledge Box:**
**Key Facts About Other Seismic Waves:**
● Primary waves are **compressional or longitudinal waves** in which particles of the medium move **in the same direction** as the wave’s propagation.
○ They are the **fastest seismic waves**, travelling at speeds of about **5–7 km/s in the** **Earth’s crust** and up to **8 km/s in the mantle**. ○ Because of their high velocity, P waves are the **first to reach a seismograph** after an earthquake occurs. ○ These waves can **travel through solids, liquids, and gases**, making them highly significant for studying the Earth's interior.
●  Secondary waves are **transverse waves**, meaning that particles move **perpendicular to** **the direction** of wave travel.
○ They are slower than P waves, moving at about **3–4 km/s in the crust** and around
**4.5 km/s in the mantle**. ○ Unlike P waves, S waves **cannot pass through liquids**, so they do not travel through the Earth’s **outer core**. This property helps scientists understand that the outer core is in a **liquid state**.
● **Strike-slip fault:** ○ It occurs in an area where two plates are sliding past horizontally with little to no vertical movement. ○ Strike-slip faults are found in California, the San Andreas Fault being the most famous,
which has caused many powerful earthquakes.
● **Normal fault:** ○ Normal faults are cracks where one mass of rock slides downward and pulls away from another mass of rock. ○ Normal faults create space. Two blocks of crust pull apart, stretching the crust into a valley. ○ The Basin and Range Province in North America and the East African Rift Zone are two well-known regions where normal faults are spreading apart Earth’s crust.
● **Reverse faults:** ○ Reverse faults, also called thrust faults, slide one block of crust on top of another. ○ This involves upward movement as the two plates collide and buckle upwards. ○ These faults are commonly found in collision zones, where tectonic plates push up mountain ranges such as the Himalayas and the Rocky Mountains.

questions.subject: Geography
questions.questionText:
With reference to the evaporation of water, consider the following statements:
I. - The rate of evaporation is greater over salt water than over fresh water.
II. 	 Low atmospheric pressure leads to a higher rate of evaporation.
III. 	 The highest annual evaporation over oceans occurs in the subtropics of the western North Atlantic and the western North Pacific.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Evaporation** is the process through which **water changes from the liquid phase to the vapour** **phase**. It is influenced by factors such as **temperature, humidity, wind speed, atmospheric** **pressure**, and **salinity**. Over oceans, evaporation forms the basis for **global water and energy cycles** and contributes to **cloud formation** and **precipitation patterns.**
● **Evaporation is lower over salt water** than fresh water because dissolved salts reduce the vapour pressure of water.
