Water molecules require **more energy to escape the surface.** Hence, saline water **evaporates more slowly** than fresh water under the same physical conditions. **So, statement** **I is not correct.**
● **Lower atmospheric pressure** reduces the **resistance** against **escaping water vapour,** making it easier for water molecules to enter the air. Therefore, low-pressure conditions **enhance evaporation,** especially when accompanied by high temperatures and low humidity. **So, statement II is correct.**
● The **highest annual evaporation** occurs over the **subtropical high-pressure belts**, particularly over the **western parts** of the **North Atlantic and North Pacific**, where warm western boundary currents (Gulf Stream and Kuroshio) raise sea surface temperatures and promote evaporation. **So,** **statement III is correct.** **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Geography
questions.questionText:
With reference to sedimentary rocks, consider the following statements:
I. - They are stratified, showing distinct layers formed by the deposition of sediments over time.
II. 	 Ice-deposited sedimentary rocks are called tillite, while wind-deposited sediments are known as loess.
III. 	 Argillaceous sedimentary rocks form the best reservoirs for groundwater and petroleum.
Which of the statements given above are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Sedimentary rocks** are formed by the **accumulation and lithification of sediments** derived from the weathering and erosion of pre-existing rocks. These sediments are transported by natural agents such as water, wind, or ice and deposited in layers over time. They are broadly classified into **mechanical (clastic), chemical, and organic** types depending on their origin and composition.
● Sedimentary rocks are **characteristically stratified**, meaning they are formed in **distinct layers** **(strata)** as sediments settle over time in water bodies like rivers, lakes, and oceans. Each layer represents a particular period of deposition and may differ in color, texture, or composition from the layers above and below. **So, statement I is correct.**
● Sedimentary rocks are also classified based on the **transporting and depositing agent**. **When ice** **acts as the depositional agent, the resulting rocks are called tillites**, formed from glacial till
which is a mix of clay, sand, and boulders left behind by retreating glaciers. **When wind acts as the** **agent, the fine-grained sediments it deposits are known as loess,** which consists mainly of siltsized particles. Loess deposits, such as those found in **northern China and central Europe**, are fertile and porous. **So, statement II is correct.**
● **Arenaceous sedimentary rocks** are characterized by a high sand content and relatively coarse particles, making them hard and porous**. Their porosity renders them excellent reservoirs for** **fluids such as groundwater and petroleum. Example: sandstone. So, statement III is not** **correct.**
● **Argillaceous sedimentary rocks**, in contrast, contain a higher proportion of clay, are fine-grained and softer, and **are generally impermeable** due to their very small or absent pore spaces. **Examples****:** **claystone and shale, which are predominantly argillaceous.** **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Geography
questions.questionText: In the context of the geography of India, Reh, Karl and Chopan are the names of:
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The **major groups of soils in India** include: Alluvial soils, Black soils, Red soils, Laterite and Lateritic soils, Forest and Mountain soils, Arid and Desert soils, Saline and Alkaline soils, Peaty and Marshy soils.
● **Saline and Alkaline Soils** are found in **Andhra Pradesh** and **Karnataka**. In the **drier parts** of **Bihar, Uttar Pradesh,** **Haryana, Punjab, Rajasthan, and Maharashtra**, there are salt-impregnated or alkaline soils that occupy 68,000 sq km of area.
● These soils are known by different names such as **reh, kallar,** **usar, thur, rakar, karl, and chopan**.
● There are many **undecomposed rock and mineral** **fragments** in these soils which, on **weathering**, **liberate sodium, magnesium and calcium salts** as well as **sulfurous acid**.
● The **accumulation of these salts** makes the soil **infertile** and **unfit for agriculture**. It has been estimated **significant portion of land** in **Uttar Pradesh** and **Punjab** have been **affected by Usar**.
● In **Gujarat**, the **area around the Gulf of Khambhat** is affected by **sea tides carrying salt-laden** **deposits**. **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: International Relations
questions.microTopic: Places in News and Geopolitical Hotspots (Ukraine, Taiwan Strait, Hormuz, etc.)
questions.questionText:
Which of the following countries shares land borders with Israel?
I. - Egypt
II. 	 Lebanon
III. 	 Jordan
IV. 	 Syria
V. 	 Saudi Arabia
Select the correct answer using the code given below:

options.a: I, II, III and IV only
options.d: I, III, IV and V only

questions.explanationMarkdown:
● Israel is located on the eastern shore of the Mediterranean Sea. It shares borders with several countries in the **Levant and North Africa** **regions.**
● Israel shares its **southwestern border with Egypt**, specifically along the **Sinai Peninsula**. The two countries are connected through the **Taba border crossing** near the Gulf of Aqaba. **So, point I is correct.**
● To the **north**, Israel shares a border with **Lebanon**, marked by the **Blue** **Line**, a UN-demarcated boundary. This border has also been a sensitive zone due to tensions between Israel and the Lebanese militant group **Hezbollah**. **So, point II is correct.**
● Israel’s **eastern border** is shared with **Jordan**, mainly along the **Jordan River and the Dead Sea**. **So, point III is correct.**
● To the **northeast**, Israel borders **Syria**. The key disputed area along this border is the **Golan Heights**,
which Israel captured from Syria during the **1967, Six-Day War** and later annexed in 1981 (a move not recognized internationally). The area remains strategically significant and has been a flashpoint in Israel–Syria relations. **So, point IV is correct.**
● Israel does **not share a direct land border** with **Saudi Arabia**. Although both countries are geographically close, separated only by the **Gulf of Aqaba**. **So, point V is not correct**. **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Geography
questions.microTopic: Physiography of India (Location, Division, Physiographic Regions)
questions.questionText:
With reference to the physiography of India, consider the following statements:
I. - The Malwa Plateau is primarily composed of basaltic lava flows.
II. 	 The erosional activity of rivers provides a rolling plain-like appearance to the Marwar Plateau.
III. 	 The Chotanagpur Plateau is predominantly composed of Gondwana rocks.
Which of the statements given above are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The Great Indian plateau is made up of a series of plateaus such as the Hazaribagh plateau, the Palamu plateau, the Ranchi plateau, the Malwa plateau, the Baghelkhand plateau, etc.
● The **Great Indian Plateau** lies to the South of the Great Northern Plains. It is **part of an ancient land mass called the** **Gondwana land.** River Narmada divides the plateau into two main parts: The central highlands and the Deccan Plateau. An extension of this plateau is also found in the northeast, in the form of Shillong and Karbi-Anglong plateau.
○ The **Central Highlands** extends between the Narmada river and the northern plains. **The Malwa Plateau, the Bundelkhand, the Baghelkhand** **plateau and the Chhota Nagpur plateau** are part of the central highlands. ○ The **Deccan Plateau** is a triangular-shaped plateau that lies to the south of the Narmada river. It is bordered by the Western Ghats in the west and the Eastern Ghats in the east.
● **The Malwa Plateau i**s a **part of the Central Highlands**. It is bordered by the Aravallis in the north, the Vindhyan Range in the south and the Bundelkhand Plateau in the east. The **Malwa Plateau is of** **volcanic origin, chiefly formed from Deccan Traps lava flows. So, statement I is correct.**
○ The Deccan Traps is a large deposition of igneous rocks in west-central India. They were formed as a result of the eruption of basaltic lava from deep inside the Earth. **Marwar Plateau or** **Mewar Plateau** is situated to the east of the Aravalli Range. Geologically, it consists of sandstone, shales, and limestones from the Vindhyan period. **Erosional activity by rivers such** **as the Banas has shaped the plateau's terrain, giving it a rolling plain-like appearance.** **So, statement II is correct.**
● **Chotanagpur Plateau** is the northeastern part of the Peninsula plateau. The Chotanagpur Plateau encompasses primarily Jharkhand, northern Chhattisgarh, and portions of West Bengal. **The plateau is** **predominantly composed of Gondwana rocks. So, statement III is correct.** **Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.**
**Knowledge Box**
**About other Plateaus:**
● **Bundelkhand Upland:** It lies to the south of the Yamuna River and to the north of the Malwa plateau. It is characterized by granite and gneiss formations, dissected by numerous deep valleys.
● **Meghalaya Plateau:** It is an extension of the peninsular plateau towards the east. It is separated from the main block by the Garo-Rajmahal Gap.
● **Maharashtra Plateau:** It is part of the larger Deccan Plateau. Basaltic rocks of lava origin underlie much of the region. The plateau has a rolling plain appearance due to weathering,
● **Dandakaranya Plateau:** It is located in the Bastar region of Chhattisgarh.
● **Telangana plateau**: It is made up of Archaean gneisses and Dharwar rocks. The Godavari valley in the plateau has Gondwana rocks and coal fields.
● **Karnataka Plateau:** It is located south of the Maharashtra Plateau. The plateau looks like a rolling plateau with an average elevation of 600-900 m. It is highly dissected by numerous rivers rising from the Western Ghats.

questions.subject: Geography
questions.questionText: Which of the following countries has territories that extend into the Arctic Circle?

options.c: Russia, United States of America, Iceland and Finland
options.d: Sweden, Poland, the United Kingdom, Iceland and Denmark

questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The **Arctic Circle** is located at approximately **66.5° north** **of the Equator**. It marks the southernmost point where, for at least one day each year, there is **24-hour daylight** **(Midnight Sun)** in summer. The region north of this circle is known as the **Arctic Region**. It holds great environmental, strategic, and economic importance due to its **climate influence, biodiversity and energy** **resources** like oil, gas, and rare minerals.
● The **Arctic Circle** crosses through **Norway, Sweden,** **Finland, Russia, the United States of America,** **Canada, Denmark (via Greenland), and Iceland**. Together, these countries form part of the **eight** **Arctic nations** recognized by the Arctic Council**.** **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Geography
questions.questionText:
Which one of the following most appropriately
describes the ‘Double Sunrise’ phenomenon?

options.a:
An astronomical event in which two stars
come very close, causing their planets to
receive light from both
options.b:
An optical illusion observed mainly during
partial solar eclipses, when refraction,
reflection, and shadowing align
options.c:
A geographical event when the Sun is
overhead the Tropic of Cancer or the Tropic
of Capricorn
options.d:
An optical phenomenon occurring along the
International Date Line due to time zone
differences

questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Double sunrise is an optical illusion,** not an astronomical event. It is **generally seen during partial** **eclipses when refraction, reflection and shadowing align perfectly.**
● Similar phenomena include sun dogs, caused by ice crystals in the atmosphere, and halo effects around the Sun or Moon. While visually similar to mirages, double sunrise is eclipse-dependent and far rarer to observe. It results as the silhouette of the moon makes the rising **sun look like two separate "horns"** **emerging from the horizon**.
● It is a rare visual event, typically occurring when the alignment of celestial bodies, especially during a **solar eclipse, interacts with Earth's atmosphere to create an optical illusion of two suns.** The second “sun” is not a real celestial object but a refracted or partially eclipsed image.
● **Reflection from calm water,** such as rivers or lakes, can intensify the illusion by mirroring the sun’s light and making the **secondary light source (actual or refracted)** appear more vivid. **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Geography
questions.microTopic: Interior of Earth and Continental Theories
questions.questionText:
With reference to Earth’s Magnetism, consider the
following statements:
I. - Convection in the inner core, combined
with the Coriolis effect, generates Earth’s
magnetic field.
II. 	 Polar wandering refers to the gradual
migration of Earth’s magnetic poles across
its surface through geologic time.
Which of the statements given above is/are
correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The **magnetic poles** are the points where the **magnetic field lines** enter or exit the Earth’s surface.
● The **magnetic field lines** of Earth resemble those of a **hypothetical magnetic dipole** located at the **centre of the Earth**. The **dipole axis** is **tilted by about 11.3°** with respect to the **Earth’s** **rotational axis**.
● **Polar wandering refers to the gradual migration of Earth’s magnetic poles across its surface** **through geologic time.**  **So, statement II is correct.**
● The **Earth possesses a magnetic field**, and the **strength** of this magnetic field **varies from place** **to place** on its surface. The **explanation** attributes the magnetic field to the **dynamo effect**.
○ According to this theory, **electrical currents** generated by the **convective motion of** **metallic fluids**, mainly **molten iron and nickel**, in the **outer core (not the inner core)** of the Earth, produce the magnetic field. **So, statement I is not correct.** **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Geography
questions.microTopic: Straits, Channels, Oceans, Seas, Gulfs, Bays
questions.questionText:
Consider the following information:
Strait Located Between Connects
I. - Makassar Borneo and Sulawesi Celebes Sea and Java Sea
II. - Hormuz Oman and Iran
Persian Gulf and Gulf of Oman
III. - Malacca Malaysia and Indonesia
Andaman Sea and South China Sea
IV. - Taiwan Taiwan and the Philippines
Philippine Sea and South China Sea
In how many of the above rows is the given
information correctly matched?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Straits** are narrow passages of water that connect two larger water bodies, such as seas or oceans. They are of great **strategic, economic, and ecological importance** because many global shipping routes pass through them.
● The **Makassar Strait** lies **between the Borneo and** **Sulawesi islands** in **Indonesia**. It connects the **Celebes** **Sea (to the north)** and the **Java Sea (to the south)**. It is an important route for ships traveling between the Pacific and Indian Oceans via the Indonesian archipelago.  **So, row** **I is correctly matched.**
● The **Strait of Hormuz** is located **between Oman and** **Iran**. It connects the **Persian Gulf** with the **Gulf of Oman**,
which further opens into the Arabian Sea. It is one of the most critical chokepoints in the world for **global oil** **transportation**, as a major portion of the world’s crude oil passes through it. **So, row II is correctly matched.**
● The **Malacca Strait connects the Andaman Sea and** **the South China Sea.** It runs between the **Indonesian** **island of Sumatra** to the west and peninsular (West) **Malaysia** and extreme southern **Thailand** to the east. **So, pair III is correctly matched.**
○ It links the Indian and Pacific oceans and is one of the busiest and most important shipping lanes in the world. ○ Singapore, Malaysia, and Indonesia control the joint patrolling of the Malacca Strait.
● The **Strait of Taiwan** lies **between Taiwan and Mainland China**. It connects the **East China Sea** **and the South China Sea**. **So, pair IV is not correctly matched.** **So, only three of the above rows are correctly matched.** **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer**.
**Knowledge Box**
**Other important straits of the world**
● **Bering Strait:** Connects the Bering Sea (Pacific Ocean) with the Chukchi Sea (Arctic Ocean).
● **Dardanelles Strait (Hellespont):**  Connects the Aegean Sea with the Sea of Marmara, and further via the Bosporus to the Black Sea.
● **Yucatán Strait**: Lies between Mexico and Cuba. It connects the Gulf of Mexico and the Caribbean Sea.
● **Cook Strait:** Separates the North and South Islands of New Zealand. It connects the Tasman Sea with the South Pacific Ocean.
● **Bab-el-Mandeb Strait**: Lies between Yemen and Djibouti/Eritrea. It connects the Red Sea with the Gulf of Aden.
● **Ten Degree Channel**: Lies between the Andaman Islands and Nicobar Islands (India). It connects the Andaman Sea with the Bay of Bengal.
● **Sunda Strait:** Lies between Java and Sumatra (Indonesia). It connects the Indian Ocean with the Java Sea.
● **The Strait of Gibraltar:** It separates Spain (Europe) and Morocco (Africa). It connects the Atlantic Ocean with the Mediterranean Sea.
● **Bosporus Strait:** In Turkey, it divides Europe and Asia. It connects the Black Sea with the Sea of Marmara.
● **Davis Strait:** Lies between Greenland and Canada. It connects the Labrador Sea with Baffin Bay.

questions.subject: Geography
questions.microTopic: Straits, Channels, Oceans, Seas, Gulfs, Bays
questions.questionText:
Consider the following islands:
I. - Andaman and Nicobar
II. 	 Maldives
III. 	 Seychelles
IV. 	 Marshall
How many of the islands given above are coral
islands?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Coral reefs** are marine structures formed by the calcium carbonate skeletons of tiny living organisms called **coral polyps**. These reefs thrive in **shallow, warm, and clear tropical waters**, typically between 30°N and 30°S latitudes.
● **While the Andaman and Nicobar Islands have both fringing and barrier reefs**, they are not classified as corral islands. They are the extension of the Arakan Yoma range of Myanmar.  The West coast of the Andaman Islands has the barrier reef, which is recognised among the other barrier reefs like those of New Caledonia, Belize, Fiji and Western Australia. These barrier reefs protect the islands from strong ocean waves and support diverse marine life. **So, point I is not correct.**
● **The Maldives consist of a chain of coral atolls formed upon the submerged peaks of an** **ancient volcanic mountain range.** The islands are low-lying, with barrier reefs surrounding the atolls providing natural protection against the erosive impact of monsoons. **So, point II is correct.**
● **The Seychelles islands,** located in the Indian Ocean, are characterized mainly by **fringing reefs** surrounding their granitic islands. These reefs grow close to shorelines and are rich in marine biodiversity. **So, point III is correct.**
● **The Marshall Islands** in the Pacific Ocean consist almost entirely of **atolls** and coral islands. Like the Maldives, they formed from submerged volcanoes surrounded by coral reefs, creating ring-shaped lagoons. The Atolls of the Marshall Islands together make up more than 10 percent of all the atolls in the world. **So, point IV is correct.** **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer**.
**Knowledge Box**
● Based on structure and relationship with nearby landmasses, coral reefs are broadly classified into **three** **main types** (proposed by Charles Darwin):
○ **Fringing** **Reef:** **Fringing reefs** grow directly from the shore, forming a narrow belt (1–2 km wide) separated from land by a shallow lagoon. They slope steeply into the deep sea, with coral growth limited outward due to the abrupt increase in depth. (e.g., **Seychelles**). ○ **Barrier Reef:** These are extensive linear reef systems that **run parallel to a** **coastline, separated from it by a lagoon**. They are the **largest type of** **coral reef**, often extending for hundreds of kilometres and several kilometres in width, forming a **broken or irregular ring** around a continent or island.(e.g., **Great Barrier Reef in Australia**). ○ **Atoll:** It is a **ring-shaped (annular) oceanic reef system encircling a** **large, often deep central lagoon.** The lagoon, typically 80–150 metres deep, is connected to the open sea through several channels that cut across the surrounding reef. (e.g., **Maldives, Marshall Islands**).

questions.subject: Geography
questions.microTopic: Minerals and Resources (Coal, Iron, Bauxite, Lead, Zinc, Pyrites, etc.)
questions.questionText:
Which one of the following countries has the
largest Mica reserves in the world?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● India’s **mica reserves are among the largest in the world,** and are of a better grade than those found in other countries with large reserves, such as China and Brazil.
● **Mica** is mainly used in the **electrical and electronic industries** because it can be **split into very** **thin sheets** that are **tough and flexible**.
● **India has a near monopoly in the production of mica**, **producing** about **60** per cent of the **world's total production.**
● In India, mica is primarily produced in **Jharkhand, Andhra Pradesh, Telangana and Rajasthan**, followed by **Tamil Nadu, West Bengal and Madhya Pradesh**.
● In **Jharkhand**, high-quality mica occurs in a **150 km long and 22 km wide belt** in the **Lower** **Hazaribagh Plateau**.
● In **Andhra Pradesh**, **Nellore district** produces the **best quality mica**. In **Rajasthan**, the mica belt extends about **320 km** from **Jaipur to Bhilwara** and around **Udaipur**.
● Mica deposits are also found in **Mysuru and Hassan districts** of Karnataka, **Coimbatore,** **Tiruchirappalli, Madurai and Kanniyakumari** in Tamil Nadu, **Alleppey** in Kerala, **Ratnagiri** in Maharashtra, and **Purulia and Bankura** districts in West Bengal. **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**
**Relevance:** The Ministry of Mines has reclassified Barytes, Felspar, Mica, and Quartz from minor minerals to major minerals.

questions.subject: Agriculture
questions.questionText:
Consider the following information:
Crop Type Largest Producing State
I. - Cotton Rabi Maharashtra
II. - Gram Rabi Madhya Pradesh
III. - Maize Kharif Karnataka
IV. - Soyabean Kharif Andhra Pradesh
In how many of the above rows is the given
information correctly matched?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Agriculture in India** is largely divided into **Kharif (monsoon)** and **Rabi (winter)** seasons. Kharif crops are sown with the onset of the **southwest monsoon (June–July)** and harvested in October– November, while Rabi crops are sown in **winter (October–December)** and harvested in spring (March–April).
● **Cotton** is a **Kharif crop**, not a Rabi crop. It requires high temperatures and a long frost-free period, thriving in **black soil regions. Major producers** include **Gujarat**, **Maharashtra**, **Telangana**, and Madhya Pradesh. Gujarat leads in production **(as per Economic Survey 2024–25). So, row I is not** **correctly matched.**
● **Gram (chana) is a Rabi pulse crop,** grown in the winter season under residual soil moisture. **Madhya Pradesh** is the **largest** producer of gram, followed by Maharashtra and Rajasthan. **So, row** **II is correctly matched.**
○ **Chickpea (Gram), Lentils, Peas, Pigeon Pea, Kulthi (Horse Gram), Masoor (Red** **Lentil), Lobias (Lobia Beans) are the pulses which are grown in the rabi season.** ○ **Green Gram (Moong), Black Gram (Urad), and Pigeon Pea (Toor) are grown during** **the kharif season.**
● **Maize (corn)** is mainly a **Kharif** crop, though some areas also grow it in Rabi. The **largest** producing state is **Karnataka**, followed by Bihar and Madhya Pradesh (Economic Survey 2024–25). **So, row III is** **correctly matched.**
● **Soybean, along with groundnut,** is a **Kharif** oilseed crop and requires warm temperatures and moderate rainfall. **Madhya Pradesh is the largest producer,** contributing around 50% of India’s total production, followed by Maharashtra and Rajasthan. **So, row IV is not correctly matched.**
○ **Mustard, Linseed, and Sesame are grown in the rabi season.**
● **Rice, Maize, Sorghum (Jowar), Bajra (Pearl Millet) are examples of kharif crops, whereas** **Wheat, Barley, Oats are the rabi crops.** **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Geography
questions.questionText:
Recently, Cyclone Montha made its landfall along
which coast of India?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Cyclone Montha** was a **Severe Cyclonic Storm** that formed over the **west-central Bay of Bengal** **(BoB)** as a low-pressure system. It made **landfall in coastal Andhra Pradesh**, causing heavy rainfall, strong winds, and extensive damage across Andhra Pradesh, Odisha, and parts of **Tamil Nadu**. **So,** **statement I is correct.**
● The cyclone was **named by Thailand** under the **World Meteorological Organization (WMO)** – Economic and Social Commission for Asia and the Pacific (ESCAP) Panel on Tropical Cyclones (PTC). The word ‘Montha’ in Thai means a **beautiful or fragrant flower. So, statement II is not correct.** **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**
**Knowledge Box**
**1. Conditions Required for the Formation of Tropical Cyclones:**
● **Warm sea surface temperature (≥ 27°C)** — provides the latent heat of condensation, which is the primary energy source.
● **Coriolis force** — necessary for cyclonic circulation; hence cyclones do not form near the equator (within 5° latitude).
● **Pre-existing low-pressure area** — acts as a seed disturbance for cyclone development.
● **High humidity in the lower and middle troposphere** — supports cloud formation and storm intensification.
● **Low vertical wind shear** — ensures organized circulation; strong shear disrupts cyclone structure.
**2. Naming of Tropical Cyclones:**
● Names are decided by the **WMO–ESCAP Panel on Tropical Cyclones (PTC)**, established in **1972**.
● **Member countries** of the North Indian Ocean region (13 nations, including India, Thailand, Bangladesh, Oman, etc.) **submit lists of names** in advance.
**Relevance:** Recently, the cyclone Montha made landfall on the Andhra Pradesh coast, India, between Machilipatnam and Kalingapatnam, near Kakinada.

questions.questionText:
What is the correct sequence of the following cities according to their latitudinal distance from the Tropic of Cancer, starting from the nearest to the farthest?
I. - Udaipur
II. 	 Kolkata
III. 	 Delhi
IV. 	 Aizawl
Select the correct answer using the code given below:
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The **Tropic of Cancer**, located at approximately **23°27****′** **N latitude**, marks the northernmost point on Earth where the Sun can appear directly overhead. This latitude corresponds to the Sun’s maximum **northern declination** relative to the celestial equator. Each year, around **June 21**—the **summer** **solstice** in the Northern Hemisphere—the Sun reaches this maximum declination and is positioned directly above the Tropic of Cancer.
● Coordinates of cities: ○ **Aizwal 23.7307° N,** 92.7173° E
○ **Kolkata: 22.5744**  **N**, 88.3629  E ○ **Udaipur: 24.5854° N**, 73.7125° E ○ **Delhi: 28.7041° N,** 77.1025° E So, the correct sequence of the cities according to their latitudinal distance from the Tropic of Cancer, starting from the nearest to the farthest is: **Aizawl, Kolkata, Udaipur** and **Delhi.** **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Agriculture
questions.questionText:
With reference to Makhana (Foxnut), consider the following statements:
I. - It is native to the Southeast Asian region.
II. 	 It is usually cultivated in still or stagnant water bodies such as ponds, lakes, and swamps.
III. 	 India contributes more than three-fourths of the global production of Makhana.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Makhana**, also known as **foxnut or gorgon nut (Euryale ferox)**, is an important **aquatic cash crop** cultivated in **still or stagnant water bodies** such as **ponds, lakes, swamps,** and oxbow lakes. Traditionally rooted in the **Mithila region of Bihar**, it holds cultural, nutritional, and economic significance. **So, statement II is correct.**
● It is considered **native to South-East Asia and China**, though it is cultivated across tropical and subtropical regions of the world. Historically, its use **as both food and medicine** dates back to ancient China and later spread across India, Japan, Malaysia, Thailand, and Nepal.  **So, statement I is correct.**
● Makhana thrives in **tropical and subtropical climates**, requiring temperatures between **20°C and** **35°C**, relative **humidity of 50–90%**, and annual **rainfall of 100–250 cm.** It grows best in smooth loamy soils under stagnant perennial water bodies. **Bihar, West Bengal, Assam, Manipur, Tripura**, and **Uttar Pradesh** provide ideal agro-climatic conditions for its cultivation.
● **India today dominates** global Makhana production, contributing **over 90% of the total world** **output**. **Bihar** alone accounts for **more than 80%** of India’s domestic production, followed by smaller outputs from West Bengal, Assam, and Uttar Pradesh. **So, statement III is correct.** **Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Geography
questions.microTopic: Pressure, Winds, Airmasses (Coriolis Force, Frictional Force, etc.)
questions.questionText:
With reference to the jet streams, consider the following statements:
I. - These are the high-velocity, geostrophic air streams that flow in a narrow band around the Earth.
II.	 They generally flow in the upper levels of the troposphere.
III.	 When the temperature contrast between air masses is maximum, the jet stream tends to flow in a nearly straight path.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Jet streams** are **upper tropospheric, high-velocity, geostrophic winds** formed due to steep pressure and temperature **gradients between air masses,** particularly along the polar front. Their speeds generally range between 110–185 km/h, but can exceed 300 km/h in stronger systems. **So,** **statement I is correct.**
○ They play a crucial role in **influencing global weather systems,** including the movement of cyclones, western disturbances, and the onset of monsoons. The main jet streams include the **subtropical westerly jet** and the **polar front jet** in both hemispheres.
● The **temperature contrast** between adjoining **air masses** (e.g., tropical and polar air) determines the strength and alignment of jet streams. When this c**ontrast is maximum**, especially during winter, the jet stream’s **velocity is highest** and it **flows in a nearly straight zonal path.** Conversely, when the contrast weakens, the jet stream becomes meandering or wavy, forming **Rossby waves**. **So,** **statement III is correct.**
● Jet streams are **generally found in the upper levels of the troposphere**, right below the tropopause.  **So, statement II is correct.** **Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Geography
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements with regard to
natural gas:
I. - It is mainly a mixture of methane with small
amounts of ethane and other hydrocarbons.
II. 	 More than 50% of India’s natural gas
production comes from offshore fields.
III. 	 Gujarat is the largest onshore producer of
natural gas in India.
IV. 	 India has set a target to increase the share
of natural gas in the energy mix to 15% by
2030.
Which of the statements given above are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Natural gas** is an important fossil fuel because it can be easily transported through pipelines.It is a **hydrocarbon mixture** mainly composed of **saturated light paraffins** such as **methane** and **ethane**, which remain **gaseous under atmospheric conditions**. It may also contain **propane, butane, pentane, and** **hexane**, and even these heavier hydrocarbons stay **gaseous in reservoirs** because of **high pressure**. **So,** **statement I is correct.**
● **Natural gas production in India increased by 5.1% in 2023**, marking the **third consecutive year** **of growth**. Total production reached **over 1.2 trillion cubic feet (Tcf)**, the **highest level since** **2012**.
● During **FY 2022–23**, **offshore fields contributed 68%** of India’s natural gas output, while **onshore** **fields accounted for 32%**.  **So, statement II is correct.**
● **Assam** was the **largest onshore producer,** contributing nearly one-third of onshore natural gas production**,** followed by Rajasthan. **So, statement III is not correct.**
● The Government has set a target to **increase the share of natural gas** in India’s energy **mix from** **about 6% at present to 15% by 2030.** **So, statement IV is correct.**
● It is stored under **high pressure as Compressed Natural Gas (CNG)**, which is used for power generation and as a cleaner fuel for transport vehicles, since it causes less pollution.
● Natural gas also serves as an **input** in the manufacture of **chemicals and fertilizers**.
● India has **significant reserves** of natural gas, and major deposits are located in **Tripura**, **Rajasthan**, **Maharashtra**, and the **Krishna-Godavari delta**.
○ Mumbai High is India’s largest offshore oil and natural gas field. **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Environment
questions.microTopic: National Park, Wildlife Sanctuary, Biosphere Reserve
questions.questionText:
Consider the following pairs:
Protected Area River Flowing Through It
I. - Panna National Park Betwa
II. - Gandhi Sagar Wildlife Sanctuary Chambal
III. - Bhitarkanika National Park Baitarani
IV. - Nameri Tiger Reserve Barak
Which of the pairs given above are correctly
matched?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Panna National Park and Tiger Reserve** is located in Vindhya Hill in Madhya Pradesh. It is the **land** **of the Ken River**. The main forest here is **Deciduous forest,** mainly teak forest & Teak-Kardhai mixed forest. Panna Tiger Reserve was included in the **global network of biosphere reserves** by the United Nations Educational, Scientific and Cultural Organisation (UNESCO) in 2020. **So, pair I is not** **correctly matched.**
○ **Ken flows through Panna Tiger Reserve.** ○ It flows through two states, namely Madhya Pradesh and Uttar Pradesh. ○ The river is the last tributary of the Yamuna before the Yamuna joins the Ganga. ○ It **originates** on the **north-west slopes of the Kaimur Range**. ○ It is **known for its rare Sajhar or Dendritic Agate stone.**
● **The Gandhi Sagar Wildlife Sanctuary** is spread across western Madhya Pradesh. It features a diverse ecosystem of savanna, open grasslands, dry deciduous forests, and riverine areas. It is recognized as an Important Bird and Biodiversity Area (IBA).
○ **River Chambal bisects the Sanctuary into almost two equal halves** and portions of the southern side is bounded by the reservoir of the Gandhisagar dam. **So, pair II is correctly** **matched.** ○ Chambal River **rises in the Vindhya Range** just south of Mhow, western Madhya Pradesh. ○ The river forms the boundary between Rajasthan and Madhya Pradesh before joining the Yamuna in Uttar Pradesh.
● **Bhitarkanika National Park** is located in Odisha. It is India’s **second-largest mangrove ecosystem** after the Sunderbans. Bhitarkanika has **one of the** **largest populations of endangered saltwater** **crocodiles** in India. It is the **second Ramsar site of** **the State after the Chilika Lake.** It is known for the visit of Olive Ridley turtles for mass nesting.
○ **Bhitarkanika National Park is inundated** **by the rivers Baitarani, Brahmani, and** **Dhamara.** Bhitarkanika forms the deltaic region of the river Brahmani and Baitarani. **So, pair III is correctly matched.** ○ Baitarani originates from the Gonasika Hills in Odisha.
● **The Nameri National Park and Tiger Reserve** is situated in Assam, along the foothills of Arunachal Pradesh. It constitutes the core of Nameri Tiger Reserve. It lies on the north bank landscape of the River Brahmaputra. Nameri National Park **shares its boundary with Pakhui Wildlife Sanctuary in** **Arunachal Pradesh.**
○ Nameri Tiger Reserve is **inundated by rivers Jia-Bhoreli (also known as Kameng) in the** **west and Bor-Dikorai** in the east. Jia-Bhoreli is a tributary of the Brahmaputra River. Bordikorai river is a tributary of Jia Bhoreli. ○ The Barak River does not flow through Nameri National Park. **So, pair IV is not correctly** **matched.** **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Geography
questions.microTopic: Critical and Strategic Minerals (Lithium, Cobalt, Titanium, KABIL, REEs, etc.)
questions.questionText:
With regard to cobalt, consider the following statements:
I. - The Democratic Republic of Congo is the largest producer of cobalt in the world.
II. 	 India has 100% import dependency for cobalt.
III. 	 Due to its magnetic properties, cobalt is used in making powerful magnets.
IV. 	 Cobalt-60 can be used to kill bacteria and sterilize fresh food.
Which of the statements given above are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● Cobalt is a **hard, lustrous, silver-grey metal** that is used mainly as a cathode material in lithium-ion and other types of batteries. It is primarily mined as a by-product of copper or nickel mining.
● It is also used in powerful magnets, cutting tools and high-strength alloys in the aerospace, energy and defense sectors.
● The **Democratic Republic of Congo** is the world's **largest mined cobalt producer, contributing**
**73.0% of global output.** Because of the rapid expansion of its nickel industry, Indonesia has recently emerged as the second-largest cobalt producer, accounting for 7.3% of global production. **So,** **statement I is correct**
○ Australia produces almost half of the world’s lithium, is the **second-largest producer of cobalt** and the fourth-largest producer of rare earths.**.**
● India currently **imports 100% of its lithium and cobalt requirements**, which are essential minerals for **electric vehicle battery manufacturing**.
○ **China, Belgium, the Netherlands, the US, and Japan are the major suppliers of cobalt** **to India****.** **So, statement II is correct.**
● Cobalt has **magnetic properties,** being a ferromagnetic metal; it can attract and be magnetized by powerful magnets. It is **used to create powerful, permanent magnets** for applications such as motors, speakers, and medical equipment. **So, statement III is correct.**
○ Cobalt compounds have been used since antiquity as a pigment for pottery, glass, paints and other media. It plays a crucial role in human nutrition as a component of vitamin B12.
● Gamma radiation from a radioactive source such as **cobalt-60 can be used to kill bacteria on fresh** **food and enable it to keep for longer. So, statement IV is correct.**
○ Cobalt-60 is a radioactive isotope that serves as a significant source of gamma radiation, widely utilized in the medical field for radiotherapy in cancer treatment and for the sterilization of medical equipment and supplies. **Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Geography
questions.microTopic: Interior of Earth and Continental Theories
questions.questionText:
Consider the following pairs:
Mountain Type of Plate Boundary
I. - Rockies Continent - Continent
II. - Alps Oceanic - Continent
III. - Taurus Oceaninc- Oceaninc
How many of the pairs given above are correctly matched?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The **Rocky Mountains** of North America were primarily formed due to **oceanic–continental** **convergence**, not continent–continent collision. The **North American Plate**, a major continental plate, has moved westward, whereas the **Juan de Fuca Plate**—a minor oceanic plate—and the **Pacific** **Plate—**a major oceanic plate—have drifted eastward. **These mountains constitute an extensive** **range extending from Canada to central New Mexico. So, pair I is not correctly matched.**
● The **Alps** were formed due to the **collision of two continental plates** — the **African Plate and** **the Eurasian Plate**. This **continent–continent convergence** led to intense folding, faulting, and uplift, creating the Alpine mountain system. **So, pair II is not correctly matched.**
○ Intense tectonic folding associated with this orogenic event uplifted marine sedimentary rocks through processes of thrusting and folding, giving rise to prominent mountain peaks such as Mont Blanc (4,810 m) along the French–Italian border.
● The **Taurus Mountains** of southern Turkey were formed due to the **collision between the African** **and Eurasian tectonic plates**, which is a **continent–continent convergence**. The compression and uplift resulting from this collision created the Taurus range. **So, pair III is not correctly matched.**
○ The mountains form an extensive mountain chain running roughly parallel to the Mediterranean coast. This system extends in a curved alignment from Lake Eğirdir in the west to the upper reaches of the Euphrates River in the east. **So, none of the above pairs are correctly matched.** **Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Geography
questions.questionText:
Consider the following information:
Ocean current Type Location
I. - Leeuwin Warm Western coast of Australia
II. - Agulhas Cold Western coast of South Africa
III. - Oyashio Warm Eastern Coast of Japan
IV. - Canary Cold Western Coast of North Africa In which of the above rows is the given information correctly matched?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Ocean currents** are the continuous flow of a huge amount of water in a definite direction. Ocean currents are like river flow in oceans. They represent a regular volume of water in a definite path and direction. Ocean currents can also be classified based on temperature: as cold currents and warm currents
● The **Leeuwin Current** is a **warm ocean current** that flows **southward** along the **western coast of Australia**. It originates from tropical waters near Indonesia and moves down past **Cape Leeuwin**, influencing the climate of **Western** **Australia** by bringing warm, low-salinity water. This warm current contributes to **milder coastal temperatures**, supports **coral reef ecosystems**, and influences rainfall patterns. **So, pair I is correctly matched.**
● The **Agulhas Current** is actually a **warm ocean current**, not a cold one. It flows **southward along the eastern** **coast** of **South Africa and Mozambique**, forming the **western boundary current of the Indian Ocean**. It carries **warm tropical water** from the Indian Ocean and meets the **cold Benguela Current** near the **Cape of** **Good Hope**, creating zones of upwelling and rich marine biodiversity. **So, pair II is not correctly matched.**
○ The **Agulhas leakage,** an **inflow of warm, salty water** **from the Indian Ocean into the South Atlantic**, is a crucial component of the global ocean overturning circulation. This exchange can influence the thermohaline property in the North Atlantic and potentially modulate the Atlantic Meridional Overturning Circulation (AMOC) variability on interannual to millennial timescales
● The **Oyashio Current** is a **cold subarctic current**, not a hot one. It flows **southward along the eastern coast of Japan**, bringing cold, nutrient-rich waters from the **Bering Sea and the Arctic Ocean**. It meets the **warm Kuroshio Current** near the northeastern coast of Japan, creating **upwelling zones** that are some of the world’s richest **fishing** **grounds**. **So, pair III is not correctly matched.**
● The **Canary Current** is a **cold ocean current** flowing southward along the **western coast of North** **Africa** from **Spain and Portugal to Senegal**. It forms part of the **North Atlantic Gyre**. The **North** **Atlantic Current** divides into two branches in the eastern Atlantic: the **North Atlantic Drift**, which continues northeast toward the **British Isles** and **Norway** as the **Norwegian Current**, and the **southern branch**, which flows between **Spain** and the **Azores** to form the **Canary Current**. The latter eventually joins the **North Equatorial Current**, completing the **North Atlantic circulation**. Within this gyre lies the **Sargasso Sea**, noted for its dense mats of **Sargassum seaweed** and unique oceanographic characteristics.
○ The Canary Current causes **upwelling of nutrient-rich waters**, making regions like the **Canary Islands and Moroccan coast** highly productive fishing zones. **So, pair IV** **is correctly matched.** **So, only two of the above pairs are correctly matched.**
**Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer**.

questions.subject: Geography
questions.questionText:
With reference to the Adiabatic Lapse Rate (ALR), consider the following statements:
I. - The Wet ALR is mainly associated with unstable atmospheric conditions.
II. 	 A positive ALR releases latent heat of condensation which is a major energy source for the development of thunderstorms and cyclones.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The Adiabatic Lapse Rate (ALR) refers to the rate of **temperature change** in a rising or sinking air parcel **without any heat exchange** with the environment. Rising air expands and cools adiabatically, while sinking air compresses and warms.
● The **Wet (Moist) Adiabatic Lapse Rate (WALR)** applies to saturated air parcels, where condensation releases latent heat, reducing the rate of cooling to **around 4°C/km. High moisture** **content, active condensation,** and latent heat release create instability, allowing air parcels to continue rising. Thus, WALR is strongly associated with **unstable conditions,** favouring **cloud** **formation**, **thunderstorms**, and deep convection. **So, statement I is correct.**
● A **positive ALR** means temperature decreases with height, which is essential for a rising air parcel to **cool adiabatically.** As the parcel rises, expansion-induced cooling leads to condensation, releasing **latent heat of condensation**—the major energy source for **thunderstorms**, **tornadoes**, and **tropical cyclones**. This rising, cooling, and **latent-heat–driven process** is the **first and** **fundamental step** in generating severe convective weather systems. **So, statement II is correct.** **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Geography
questions.microTopic: Critical and Strategic Minerals (Lithium, Cobalt, Titanium, KABIL, REEs, etc.)
questions.questionText:
Which of the following are the primary commercial sources of Lithium in the world?
I. - Salt lakes
II. 	 Pegmatite rocks
III. 	 Clay and borate minerals
IV. 	 Deep-sea manganese nodules
Select the correct answer using the code given below:
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Lithium** is mixed (alloyed) with aluminium and magnesium for light-weight alloys, **and is also used in** **batteries, some greases, some glasses, and in medicine. It does not** occur as the **free metal** in nature because of its high reactivity. Deposits are known all around the world.
● **Salt lakes (salars):** Lithium-rich brines from salt lakes constitute a major commercial source of lithium, particularly in **South America**. **So, point I is correct.**
● **Pegmatite rocks:** Hard-rock mining of lithium-bearing pegmatites is another important commercial source, with **Australia** being the leading producer.  **So, point II is correct.**
● **Clay and borate minerals:** These occur in **sedimentary basins** and represent commercially viable or **emerging sources of lithium**.  **So, point III is correct.**
● **Deep-sea manganese nodules:** Although these nodules contain lithium and are considered a **potential future resource**, they are **not presently exploited commercially** due to **technological, logistical, and environmental constraints**.  **So, point IV is not correct.** **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**
**Knowledge Box**
● **Lithium** and its compounds are widely used in modern technology. Lithium stearate forms **high-** **temperature lubricants**, while **lithium hydroxide** absorbs **CO**₂ **in space vehicles**.
● **Mercury** has the lowest melting point of all metals. **Cesium** has the lowest melting point among all stable metals, not lithium. **Lithium** actually has the highest melting point of all the alkali metals. **So, statement II is not correct.**
● It is alloyed with metals like aluminium to make **high-performance aircraft alloys**, and it is also used in **battery anodes** and **energy storage** due to its **high electrochemical potential**. **Lithium carbonate** is also used as a **drug** to treat **manic-depressive disorders**. **So,** **statement IV is correct.**
● It supports **heat-transfer** and **nuclear applications** because of its **high specific heat**. Lithium compounds are important **reagents in organic chemistry** and are used to make **high-** **strength glass and ceramics**.
● India currently **imports all of its lithium** from **Australia and Argentina** and nearly **70% of** **its lithium-ion cell demand** from **China and Hong Kong**. **So, statement III is correct.**
● The discovery of **lithium reserves in Jammu & Kashmir** has the potential to **support India’s** **domestic battery-manufacturing industry** and **reduce import dependence**. If further exploration confirms the **estimated size of the reserves**, India could **surpass China** in terms of its **lithium stockpile**, strengthening its position in the **global EV supply chain**.

questions.subject: Geography
questions.questionText:
Consider the following rivers:
I. - Subarnarekha
II. 	 Vaigai
III. 	 Vaitarna
IV. 	 Shetruniji
Which of the rivers given above drains into the Bay of Bengal?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The drainage system of India is broadly classified based on the direction in which rivers flow either into the **Arabian Sea** or into the **Bay of Bengal**. Approximately **77% of India’s total drainage** **area flows into the Bay of Bengal**, while the remaining **23%** **drains into the Arabian Sea**.
● The **Subarnarekha** is **one of the longest east-flowing** **inter-state rivers**. **TIt** rises near Nagri village in the Ranchi District of Jharkhand.  It drains **into the Bay of** **Bengal.** Its basin extends over  Jharkhand, Odisha and West Bengal. Its principal tributaries are the Kanchi, the Karkari and the Kharka**i. So, point I is correct.**
● **The Vaigai River** is an important river of Tamil Nadu. The river Vaigai originates in the eastern slope of the Western Ghats mountainous offshoot in the Varushanad area. Thereafter it  flows east and southeast directions till it **confluences with the Bay of Bengal.** The Suruliyar and the Manjalar are its important tributaries. **So, point II is correct.**
● The **Vaitarna River** rises from the **Trimbak hills in the Nasik district of Maharashtra**. It is a west-flowing river. It flows through  Maharashtra and finally drains into the **Arabian Sea.** **So, point III** **is not correct.**
● **The Shetruniji** is one of the major rivers of Gujarat. It rises at Chachai hills in Gir Forest of Gujarat. It flows towards the east and **empties into the Arabian Sea through the Gulf of Cambay**. **So,** **point IV is not correct.**
**Knowledge Box**
**Other Rivers that drain into the Arabian Sea:**
● **Dhadhar River** rises in Gujarat and empties into the Gulf of Khambat.
● **The Sabarmati River** originates in the Aravalli Range of Rajasthan and meets the Gulf of Khambhat of the Arabian Sea.
● **The Mahi River** originates from the Vindhyachal Hills in Madhya Pradesh and meets in the Gulf of Khambhat.
● **The Kalinadi** rises from the Belgaum district of Karnataka and falls in the Arabian Sea through Karwar Bay.
● **The Sharavati River** rises in Karnataka in the Western Ghats before reaching the Arabian Sea.
● **The Mandovi River** is one of the main west-flowing rivers of Goa State. It rises in the Jamboti Ghats and falls into the Arabian Sea.
● **The Bharathapuzha River** is the second-longest West west-flowing river that drains into the Arabian Sea in Kerala State. **Rivers that drain into the Bay of Bengal:**
● **The Baitarani** is one of the important east-flowing rivers of peninsular India, flowing eastward and joining the Bay of Bengal. The river rises in the hill ranges of Keonjhar District of Odisha.
● **The Brahmani** rises near Nagri village in Ranchi district of Jharkhand and  falls into the Bay of Bengal.
● **Vamsadhara** is an east-flowing river that originates in **Kalahandi district of Odisha.** It drains into the Bay of Bengal.
● **Pennar River** rises from the Chenna Kesava hills of the Nandi ranges of Karnataka and drains into the Bay of Bengal.
**Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Geography
questions.questionText:
With reference to Primary Rocks, consider the
following statements:
I. - These rocks are unfossiliferous.
II. 	 These are formed by lithification and cover
75 per cent of the Earth’s crust.
III. 	 Granite is a primary rock.
Which of the statements given above is/are
correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● Primary rocks, also known as **igneous rocks**, are the **earliest-formed rocks** of the Earth’s crust and serve as the **foundation for all other rock types**. They originated from the **cooling and** **solidification of molten magma** — either deep beneath the Earth’s surface (intrusive) or on the surface after volcanic eruptions (extrusive). These rocks provide the **parent material** from which **sedimentary** and **metamorphic rocks** are derived.
● Since **primary (igneous) rocks** form from the **solidification of molten magma**, the **extremely** **high temperatures** during their formation **destroy any organic matter**. As a result, **fossils** are very rare in these rocks. This is why igneous rocks such as **basalt, granite, and gabbro** are termed **unfossiliferous**. **So, statement I is correct.**
● **Lithification,** the compaction and cementation of sediments**,** is the process by which **sedimentary** **rocks** are formed**.** Although **sedimentary rocks cover about 75% of the** Earth’s surface, igneous and metamorphic rocks constitute the majority of the Earth’s crust by volume.  **So, statement II is** **not correct.**
● **Granite** is a classic example of an **intrusive igneous rock**, which forms when magma cools slowly beneath the Earth’s surface, allowing large crystals to develop. It is termed **acidic** and contains minerals such as **quartz, feldspar, and mica**. **So, statement III is correct.** **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**
**Knowledge Box**
● Based on the proportion of acid-forming radicals, particularly silica, rocks are broadly classified into acidic and basic types. **Acidic rocks possess a high silica content,** typically reaching up to 80%, and are largely composed of minerals such as quartz and feldspar. Representative examples include **granite, quartz, and feldspar-rich rocks.**
● In contrast, **basic rocks contain a relatively low proportion of silica,** averaging around 40%, but are enriched in magnesia (up to 40%) along with other constituents such as iron oxides, lime, alumina, and alkalis. Common examples of basic rocks include **basalt, gabbro, and dolerite.**

questions.subject: Geography
questions.questionText:
With regard to the Terai soil in India, consider the
following statements:
I. - It is rich in nitrogen content.
II. 	 It is poorly drained and marshy soil, occurring
in a narrow belt south of the Bhabar region.
III. 	 It is suitable for the cultivation of jute and
sugarcane.
Which of the statements given above is/are
correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The **alluvial soils** are divided into **old alluvium (Bhangar)**, **new alluvium (Khadar)**, **Bhabar**, and **Terai**. These soils are **pale in colour** and **sandy**, while being **less kankary** in nature.
○ Due to the softness of the strata and fertility, the alluvial soils are well suited to irrigation and respond very well to canal and well/tube-well irrigation. ○ If properly maintained, these soils can yield splendid crops of rice, wheat, sugarcane, tobacco, cotton, jute, maize, oilseeds, vegetables, and fruits.
● Along the Shiwalik foothills, there is a zone 8–16 km wide known as Bhabar. To the south of the Bhabar, running parallel to it, lies a long narrow strip of swampy lowland with silty soils called the Terai.
● It is **poorly drained** and **marshy** in nature, occurring in a **narrow belt south of the Bhabar region.** **So, statement II is correct.**
● The soils of this region are **rich in nitrogen and organic matter** but **deficient in phosphate**. **So,** **statement I is correct.**
● These soils are **suitable for a number of crops** such as **wheat, rice, sugarcane, jute, and soybean** under **reclaimed conditions**. **So, statement III is correct.** **Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.**
**Knowledge Box**
**Key Facts About Alluvial Soils:**
● **Khadar soils** are mainly found in **low-lying areas** that **remain flooded throughout** **the year**, whereas **Bhangar soils** are found on **higher reaches**, about **30 meters** **above the flood level**.
● **Bhangar soil** is **clayey** and **generally dark coloured**, and **below the surface** of this soil lie **beds of lime nodules** known as **kankar**. **Khadar soils** are **more fertile** than **Bhangar soils**.
● **Bhabar** **is not suitable for agriculture**, and **big trees with large roots thrive** here. The region consists of **alluvial fans** having **coarse and often pebbly soils** which merge together to form this belt.

questions.subject: Geography
questions.microTopic: Movement of Ocean Water (Tides, Waves, etc.)
questions.questionText:
Which of the following statements best describes
a Rip Current?

options.a:
A narrow current of water flowing parallel to
the shoreline, caused by longshore drift
options.b:
A strong, localized and fast-moving current
of water flowing directly away from the shore
options.c:
A vertical underwater current that brings
cold, nutrient-rich water from deeper oceanic
layers to the surface
options.d:
A deep ocean current caused by differences
in temperature and salinity

questions.explanationMarkdown:
● A **Rip Current** is a strong, narrow, and fast-moving stream of water that flows **seaward** — from the shoreline back toward the **open ocean, sea, or lake**. These currents are among the most well-known and dangerous **coastal hazards** faced by swimmers and surfers worldwide.
● Rip currents are formed primarily due to variations in **beach topography** such as **sandbars, piers, reefs**, or underwater ridges. When waves break onshore, water accumulates near the beach and must return seaward. If this returning water finds a narrow gap — between sandbars or around obstacles — it rushes back toward the sea as a concentrated, high-speed current called a **rip current**. Contrary to popular belief, rip currents do not pull people underwater; rather, they **carry them away from the shore**, often beyond the breaking waves. **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**

‘Didwana’, ‘Kuchaman’ and ‘Pachpadra’ are the

questions.subject: Geography
questions.microTopic: Minerals and Resources (Coal, Iron, Bauxite, Lead, Zinc, Pyrites, etc.)
questions.questionText:
With reference to coal in India, consider the
following statements:
I. - India has more than one-fourth of the world’s
coal reserves.
II. 	 It generally has a low sulphur content.
III. 	 It has the largest share in India’s power
generation.
IV. 	 Gondwana coal accounts for the largest
reserve and production in India.
Which of the statements given above are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Coal** is one of the principal **fossil fuels** and serves as a **primary source of energy and power**. The term **coal** is generally applied to a **sedimentary formation** that is **highly carbonaceous** in nature and is formed by the **accumulation of previously existing plant material**.
● Indian coal has **low sulphur content**, which helps reduce sulphur-related emissions. **So, statement** **II is correct.**
● The **ash content in Indian coal** typically ranges from **15% to 45%**, with most of the domestic production falling in the **35%–45%** category.
○ In contrast, **imported coal** typically has an ash content of **10%–20%**. The higher ash percentage in India’s coal is primarily due to its **drift origin**.
● **India** has the **fifth-largest coal reserves globally and is the world's second-largest consumer** **of coal**.
○ It has around **ten percent** of the world’s coal reserves**. So, statement I is not correct.**
● By 2024, **Jharkhand** remained India’s leading coal-producing state, accounting for approximately **26%** **of the nation’s total coal reserves,** estimated at around 28 billion tonnes. Located in eastern India, Jharkhand’s extensive coal deposits play a pivotal role in sustaining the country’s energy sector. **Odisha,** **holding about 25.1% of India’s coal reserves,** is similarly resource-rich, with major deposits in the Talcher and Ib Valley coalfields. **Chhattisgarh, with an 18.1% share, is emerging as a significant** **contributor** through the rapid development of its Korba, Raigarh, and Surguja coalfields, reinforcing its strategic importance in India’s coal industry.
● Coal remains crucial, contributing 55% to the national energy mix and fuelling over **74% of total power** **generation**. **So, statement III is correct.**
● **Gondwana coal accounts for approximately 98% of India’s total coal reserves** and **nearly 99%** **of its domestic coal production**. **So, statement IV is correct.**
○ The **Gondwana coal**, which is **about 250 million years old**, has **lower carbon content** as compared to **Carboniferous coal** (350 million years old), which is **almost absent in India** due to geological reasons.
● **Gondwana coalfields** produce both **coking** and **non-coking coal**, and include **bituminous** as well as **sub-bituminous** grades. **Anthracite** deposits are **generally absent** in Gondwana formations. **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**
**Knowledge Box**
**Key Facts About Coal in India:**
● Coal occurs in rock sequences belonging to **two major geological ages** — the **Gondwana** period and the **Tertiary** period.
● About **80% of India’s coal deposits** are **bituminous**, mostly of **non-coking grade**. The most significant **Gondwana coalfields** are found in the **Damodar Valley**, covering the **Jharkhand–West Bengal coal belt**, with major coalfields such as **Raniganj, Jharia, Bokaro, Giridih, and Karanpura**. **Jharia** is the **largest coalfield** in India, followed by **Raniganj**.
● Coal-bearing river valleys include the **Godavari**, **Mahanadi**, and **Sone**. Major **coal** **mining centres** of India include **Singrauli** in Madhya Pradesh (with a part extending into Uttar Pradesh), **Korba** in Chhattisgarh, **Talcher and Rampur** in Odisha, **Chanda–Wardha, Kamptee, and Bander** in Maharashtra, **Singareni** in Telangana, and **Pandur** in Andhra Pradesh.
● **Tertiary coal deposits** are primarily located in the northeastern region, comprising **Assam, Arunachal Pradesh, Meghalaya, and Nagaland**. These are extracted from **Darangiri, Cherrapunji, Mewlong, and Langrin** in Meghalaya; **Makum,** **Jaipur, and Nazira** in upper Assam; **Namchik–Namphuk** in Arunachal Pradesh; and **Kalakot** in Jammu & Kashmir.
● In addition, **brown coal (Lignite)** occurs in the **coastal areas of Tamil Nadu,** **Puducherry, Gujarat, and Jammu & Kashmir**.

questions.subject: Geography
questions.microTopic: Physiography of India (Location, Division, Physiographic Regions)
questions.questionText:
With reference to the Himalayan mountain system,
consider the following statements:
I. - The Shiwaliks are more continuous and
widespread than the Himadri.
II. 	 The Eastern Himalayas, on average, have a
higher elevation than the Western Himalayas.
Which of the statements given above is/are **not**
correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The Himalayan range is a young fold mountain. These mountain ranges run in a west-east direction from the Indus to the Brahmaputra.
● Himalaya comprises a chain of parallel mountain ranges. The three main ranges are:
○ **The Greater Himalayas** are the northernmost range known as the **Himadri.** It bears some of the highest mountain peaks (ranging from 6000 to 8000 m), including Mount Everest. **It is the most continuous range.** The average height of this range from sea level is about 6100 metres. ○ **The Lesser Himalaya or Himachal** lies to the south of the Greater Himalaya. The breadth of the Himachal range is 60 to 80 km, and the height varies from 1000 metres to 4500 metres. **The Pir Panjal, Dhauladhar, Nag Tibba and Mahabharat** **Range** are common high ranges of the Lesser Himalaya. ○ **Also known as the Outer Himalayas, the Shiwalik Range** forms the southernmost and geologically youngest section of the Himalayan mountain system. It lies between the Great Plains and the Lesser Himalayas, extending for about 2,400 km from the Potwar Plateau in Pakistan to the Brahmaputra Valley in Assam.
■ The range has an altitude of 600–1,500 metres and a width varying from about 50 km in
Himachal Pradesh to less than 15 km in Arunachal Pradesh. ■ The southern slopes are steep, while the northern slopes are gentle. ■ **The Shiwaliks form an almost continuous chain of low hills, except for a gap**
**of about 80–90 km occupied by the Tista and Raidak river valleys.** Forest cover is dense in the eastern and central sections (Nepal and Northeast India) but decreases westward due to declining rainfall. **In Punjab and Himachal Pradesh, the southern** **slopes are nearly barren and are dissected by seasonal streams known as** **Chos****.**
■ Geologically, the Shiwaliks consist of unconsolidated sediments such as sand, clay, and
conglomerates, deposited by rivers from the Himalayas. ○ **Himadri** is the only Himalaya range that **maintains its continuity from west to east. It is** **more continuous and widespread than the Shiwaliks. So, statement I is not correct.**
● **The Western Himalayas, on average, have a higher elevation than the Eastern Himalayas.** Also, the altitudinal variations are greater in the eastern half than in the western half. **So, statement II** **is not correct.**
○ In the western Himalayan ranges, elevation increases gradually as the mountains rise from the plains in a series of successive stages. Consequently, the higher mountain ranges are situated farther away from the plains. In contrast, the eastern Himalayan ranges ascend abruptly from the plains, bringing the high peaks, such as Kanchenjunga, relatively close to the plains. **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Geography
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements:
**Statement I:** Tropical and subtropical coastal deserts are primarily influenced by ocean currents.
**Statement II:** In lower latitudes, warm currents along eastern ocean shores contribute to the formation of coastal deserts.
Which one of the following is correct in respect of the above statements?

options.a: Both Statement I and Statement II are correct and Statement II explains Statement I
options.b: Both Statement I and Statement II are correct but Statement II does **not** explain Statement I
options.c: Statement I is correct but Statement II is incorrect
options.d: Statement I is incorrect but Statement II is correct

questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Ocean currents** are large-scale movements of surface water driven by winds, Earth’s rotation, and differences in water temperature and salinity. They play a crucial role in redistributing heat across the globe and thus greatly influence the **climate of coastal regions**. Generally, **warm currents** flow from the equator toward the poles, while **cold currents** move from polar to tropical regions. This pattern affects rainfall, temperature, and even the formation of **deserts** along continental coasts.
● Deserts like the **Atacama (South America)**, **Namib (Africa)**, and **Sahara (Africa)** lie along the **west coasts** of continents. They are influenced by **cold currents** (e.g., Humboldt, Benguela currents and Canary current), not warm ones. Cold currents lower evaporation and suppress rainfall, leading to **arid conditions.** **So, statement I is correct.**
● Warm currents like the **Brazil Current**, **Agulhas Current**, and **Kuroshio Current** flow along the **eastern coasts of continents** in tropical and subtropical zones, bringing **warm and humid** **conditions**. **So, statement II is not correct.** **So, statement–I is correct but Statement–II is incorrect.** **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer**

questions.subject: Geography
questions.microTopic: Water in the Atmosphere (Clouds, Condensation, Precipitation, Rainfall, etc.)
questions.questionText:
In the context of cloud-seeding, consider the following statements:
I.  	 It works on cirrus clouds only.
II. 	 Silver iodide is the most widely used chemical for cold cloud seeding.
III. 	 It requires sufficient moisture content in the clouds to succeed.
Which of the statements given above are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Cloud seeding** is a **weather-modification technique** that introduces artificial nuclei into suitable clouds to enhance **precipitation**. It is used globally for drought mitigation, increasing snowpack, or occasionally for **pollution management.** The technique requires **moisture-laden clouds,** appropriate temperatures, and specific chemical nuclei. Its **success is highly variable** and dependent on atmospheric conditions.
● Cloud seeding is **broadly of two types, cold cloud** seeding and **warm cloud** seeding.  Cold cloud seeding targets **subfreezing clouds** containing supercooled water droplets. **Silver iodide (AgI)** is most widely used because its **crystal lattice** closely **resembles natural ice,** enabling instant freezing and ice-crystal growth. Other agents include dry ice (solid CO₂) and liquid propane. **So, statement II** **is correct.**
○ **Warm cloud seeding** primarily uses **hygroscopic** **salts** like **sodium chloride** and **calcium** **chloride.** These salts attract water molecules, forming large droplets that **grow and fall as rain** **in clouds** with temperatures above freezing.
● Cloud seeding does **not work on all cloud types,** but the limitation is **not restricted only to cirrus** **clouds**. Clouds may be unsuitable for seeding **depending on moisture availability, temperature,** **vertical development**, and cloud dynamics. Only certain types, typically **cumulus**, **stratiform**, and suitable **supercooled clouds**, respond effectively to seeding. **So, statement I is not correct.**
● The **cloud-seeding attempt in Delhi failed** mainly due to a **lack of adequate moisture** in the clouds. For seeding to succeed, clouds must contain **sufficient water vapour** or supercooled droplets. **IIT-Kanpur** confirmed that although **particulate concentration** reduced slightly, rainfall could not occur because the moisture levels were too low, **preventing nucleation** and subsequent raindrop growth. **So, statement III is correct.** **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Geography
questions.microTopic: Pressure, Winds, Airmasses (Coriolis Force, Frictional Force, etc.)
questions.questionText:
With reference to the Doldrums (Equatorial LowPressure Belt), consider the following statements:
I. - It is characterized by extremely low pressure throughout the year.
II. 	 Only horizontal air currents are found in this region.
III. 	 It has zero coriolis force, preventing the formation of tropical cyclones.
Which of the statements given above are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The **Doldrums**, also known as the **Equatorial Low-Pressure Belt** or the **Inter-Tropical** **Convergence Zone (ITCZ)**, extend roughly between **10°N and 10°S**, though the width may vary with the Sun’s apparent movement. Intense **vertical heating near the equator** causes strong convection, producing a zone of **permanent low pressure,** weak surface winds, high humidity, and frequent thunderstorms.
● This belt experiences **extremely low pressure** because the equatorial region receives maximum insolation, **causing the air to heat rapidly,** expand, and rise. Persistent convection keeps **surface** **pressure low** throughout the year. Therefore, the doldrum zone is characterized by **permanent low** **pressure**. **So, statement I is correct.**
● Winds from both hemispheres **converge in this region** and begin to rise sharply, leaving **no** **significant horizontal (surface) wind movement.** As a result, the area is known for calm conditions, lack of steady winds, and the **dominance of vertical currents.** This is precisely **why** **sailors historically feared** this zone for its windless conditions. **So, statement II is not correct.**
● At the equator, the **Coriolis force approaches zero** due to the Earth s geometry. With no deflective force to initiate rotation, **tropical cyclones cannot form** in the Doldrums, despite **high** **temperatures** and abundant moisture. This is why the equatorial belt, **though ideal for convection,** is cyclone-free. **So, statement III is correct.** **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Geography
questions.questionText:
With reference to ravines, consider the following statements:
I. - They are deep, narrow gorges that are generally larger than a gully.
II. 	 They are formed primarily by lateral corrasion of mature rivers in their plain course.
III. 	 The Luni Basin and the Yamuna Basin in India exhibit significant badland or ravine topography.
Which of the statements given above is/ are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Running water** is one of the most powerful agents of erosion that shapes the Earth’s surface over time. As streams and rivers flow, they erode soil and rocks, creating a variety of erosional features that differ in size, depth, and intensity of erosion.
● A ravine is a deep, narrow gorge formed by prolonged erosion and represents an **advanced stage of** **gully erosion**, making it generally larger than a gully. **So, statement I is correct.**
● Ravines are formed mainly by **vertical erosion and headward cutting** due to surface runoff and flash floods. **Lateral corrasion** is characteristic of mature rivers in their plain course and is not the typical process responsible for ravine formation. **So, statement II is not correct.**
● The **Yamuna Basin** (notably the Chambal–Yamuna region) and the **Luni Basin** in Rajasthan exhibit extensive **badland or ravine topography** caused by intense surface erosion of soft, poorly consolidated soils under semi-arid conditions. **So, statement III is correct.** **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**
**Knowledge Box**
● A gorge is a **deep, narrow valley with steep, almost vertical walls**, usually formed by a river cutting through hard rock. It often represents an early stage of canyon formation, where **vertical erosion dominates over lateral erosion**.
● A canyon is a **very deep and wide valley** with steep sides, typically carved by a large river flowing over a long geological period through resistant rock layers. Canyons represent **the most advanced stage of fluvial erosion**, such as the Grand Canyon of the Colorado River in the USA.

questions.subject: Geography
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements:
I. - India is the world's third-largest producer of solar-generated electricity.
II. 	 India has surpassed the 50% mark for nonfossil fuel sources in its total installed electricity capacity.
III. 	 Gujarat is the largest producer of solar power in India.
Which of the statements given above is/are **not** correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● As one of the fastest-growing economies, India has set ambitious targets to ensure energy security, promote environmental health and meet its global climate commitments under the Paris Agreement. This includes a pledge to achieve **500 gigawatts (GW) of renewable** **capacity by 2030** and reach net-zero carbon emissions by 2070.
● India has become the **world’s third-largest solar energy** **producer** with an **installed capacity of about 125 GW**, as stated by the **Minister for New and Renewable Energy. So, statement I is correct.**
● India s renewable energy targets are ambitious, with a strategic approach to harness its abundant solar resources. **By 2030,** India aims to reach **280 GW of solar power,** which will form a significant portion of the country’s overall target of 500 GW of renewable energy.
● India **has achieved** one of its NDCs which was to achieve about **50% of the cumulative electric** **power installed capacity from non-fossil fuel-based energy resources** by 2030. **So, statement** **II is correct.**
○ **India’s total installed power capacity** is now **majority non-fossil fuel based**. Non-fossil fuel sources, including **renewable energy, hydro, and nuclear**, account for **256.09 GW**,
which is **over 51%** of the country’s total capacity. ○ **Solar power** has reached **127.33 GW, while fossil-fuel-based sources** indicate rapid growth in clean energy deployment.
● As of late 2025, India’s solar power landscape shows **Rajasthan maintaining its lead,** followed by **Gujarat, with Maharashtra** having risen to the third position. **So, statement III is not correct.**
● The **Bhadla Solar Park in Rajasthan,** with a capacity of 2.2 GW, is the **third largest** solar park worldwide, highlighting India’s commitment to scaling its renewable infrastructure.
● The **International Solar Alliance (ISA)** launched by **India and France in 2015** at **COP21** now has **124 member and signatory countries**, and continues to guide the **global solar agenda** through climate finance, technology, policy and skill development. **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Geography
questions.microTopic: Physiography of India (Location, Division, Physiographic Regions)
questions.questionText:
Arrange the following hills of Peninsular India in the correct sequence of their location from North to South:
I. - Shevaroy
II. 	 Kudremukh
III. 	 Anaimudi
IV. 	 Nallamala
V. 	 Doda Betta
Select the correct answer using the code given below:
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Shevaroy Hills** are located in the **Eastern Ghats.** The Shevaroy Hills are located in the **Salem district of Tamil Nadu.**
