● **Kudremukh Peak** is located in the **Western Ghats** in the Chikmagalur district of **Karnataka.** It has a marked similarity to the face of a horse. It is a part of the Kudremukh Range.
● **Anaimudi peak** (2695 m) is situated in **Kerala**. It is the highest peak of the Anaimalai hills, which is a part of the Western Ghats.
● **Nallamalla hills** are in **Andhra Pradesh**. It is a part of **the** **Eastern Ghats.**
● **Doda Betta** (2,637 m) is the **second-highest peak of South** **India.** It is situated in the Nilgiri hills. Doddabetta is the highest Peak in Tamil Nadu.
● As can be seen from the image given above, the correct sequence of the given hills as one moves from North to South is: **Nallamala** **Hills - Kudremukh Hills - Shevaroy Hills - Doda Betta -** **Anaimudi.** **So, the correct answer is IV- II- I- V- III.** **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Geography
questions.questionText:
With reference to the temperature distribution of oceans, consider the following statements:
I. - In the Arctic and Antarctic regions, a distinct thermocline layer is present throughout the year.
II. 	 Enclosed seas, both in lower and higher latitudes, tend to have relatively higher temperatures compared to open oceans.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Ocean temperature** exhibits both **horizontal variation** **with latitude** and **vertical variation with depth**. The **surface layer** receives direct **solar insolation**, making it relatively warm, while the **deeper layers** remain progressively colder. The **transition** **zone** between these layers, characterized by a **rapid decrease in temperature with** **increasing depth**, is known as the **thermocline**.
● In the **Arctic and Antarctic circles**, surface water temperatures are already close to **0°C**, leaving little temperature difference between surface and deep waters. As a result, **no** **distinct thermocline** forms in these polar regions. The water column is nearly **uniformly cold from the surface to the** **ocean floor**, forming a **single cold layer** instead of three layers (surface, thermocline, deep water) that are observed in tropical and mid-latitude oceans. **So, statement I is not correct.**
● The temperature of **enclosed seas** depends on their **latitude** and the degree of **mixing with open** **ocean waters**. In **low latitudes (tropical regions)**, enclosed seas (like the Red Sea or Persian Gulf) do record **higher temperatures** than nearby open oceans because of restricted circulation, high evaporation, and intense solar radiation. However, in **high latitudes (polar or subpolar regions)**, enclosed seas (like the Baltic Sea) actually record **lower temperatures** than open oceans. This occurs because they are surrounded by cold landmasses and have limited exchange with warmer ocean currents. **So, statement II is not correct.** **Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.**
**Knowledge Box**
● The ocean’s temperature structure in middle and low latitudes forms a three-layer system. The surface layer (up to ~500 m) consists of warm water (20–25°C), permanent in the tropics but seasonal in mid-latitudes. Beneath it lies the thermocline (500–1,000 m), marked by a sharp temperature decline. The deep layer is very cold and nearly uniform to the ocean floor.

questions.subject: Geography
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements with regard to the Oort Cloud:
I. - It is a cloud of dust that surrounds the Solar System.
II. 	 It begins beyond the Kuiper Belt, marking the outermost region of the Solar System.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● In **1950**, astronomer **Jan Oort** proposed that some comets originate from a **vast, extremely distant** **spherical shell of icy bodies (not a cloud of dust)** surrounding the **solar system**. **This enormous** **region of icy objects is now known as the Oort Cloud**, which occupies space at distances between **5,000 and 100,000 astronomical units (AU)** from the Sun. **So, statement I is not correct.**
● It is a distant and dynamic region of the Solar System that overlaps with and **extends beyond the** **Kuiper Belt. So, statement II is correct.**
● The leading theory suggests the Oort Cloud formed **4.6 billion** **years ago** when planets finished forming and many **planetesimals** remained.
● **Planetary gravity**, especially that of **Jupiter**, scattered these icy objects. Some were **ejected from the solar system**, while others were pushed into **very distant, eccentric orbits** still controlled by the Sun.
● Objects in the Oort cloud **do not share a common orbital** **plane,** can travel **above, below, and at various inclinations** around the Sun and form a **thick bubble** of **distant icy debris.**
● The Oort Cloud may contain **hundreds of billions** to **trillions** of icy bodies. When disturbed, one of these objects begins a **long** **journey** toward the Sun, becoming a **long-period comet**. **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Geography
questions.microTopic: Pressure, Winds, Airmasses (Coriolis Force, Frictional Force, etc.)
questions.questionText:
With reference to the Polar Vortex, consider the following statements:
I. - It is a circumpolar low-pressure system found at both of the Earth’s poles.
II. 	 In the Northern Hemisphere, it is a counterclockwise flow of air  that is associated with Rossby waves.
II. 	 It can lead to ozone depletion over the polar regions during winter.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The **polar vortex** is a large, **circumpolar**, **upper-tropospheric** to lower-stratospheric low-pressure system composed of **extremely cold air.** It forms over the both the poles during winter and is closely linked with the **polar jet stream. So, statement I is correct.**
● The polar vortex refers to the **counter-clockwise (cyclonic) circulation** of air in the **Northern** **Hemisphere** (and clockwise in the Southern Hemisphere) caused by the **Coriolis effect**, which helps confine cold air near the poles. Disturbances or meanders in the polar vortex and the associated jet stream are linked to atmospheric **Rossby waves. So, statement II is correct.**
● The polar vortex plays a crucial role in **ozone depletion,** especially over **Antarctica**. During polar winters, extremely cold temperatures lead to the formation of **Polar Stratospheric Clouds (PSCs)** within the vortex. These clouds convert chlorine-containing reservoir compounds into **highly reactive** **radicals (Cl, ClO),** which **rapidly destroy ozone** with the return of sunlight. The ozone hole is therefore most intense when the polar vortex is strong and **temperatures remain below** **−****78°C,** favoring PSC formation. **So, statement III is correct.** **Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Geography
questions.questionText:
With reference to rock-forming minerals, consider
the following statements:
I. - Amphibole forms nearly half of the Earth's
crust.
II.	 Cinnabar is typically found in areas of
geologically recent volcanic activity,
commonly associated with geysers and
fumaroles.
III. 	 Granitic pegmatites are the most common
and economically important source of high-
quality industrial feldspar.
Which of the statements given above is/are
correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Rock-forming minerals** are naturally occurring inorganic substances that constitute the majority of Earth’s crust. They play key roles in various geological processes and also have significant industrial and economic importance. **Common rock-forming minerals include silicates like feldspar, quartz,** **and amphibole, and non-silicates like calcite and hematite.**
● **Amphibole** is a group of minerals that occur in metamorphic and igneous rocks. However, it **does not** **form nearly half of the Earth's crust**. The majority of the Earth's crust is composed of **feldspar** **(around 50%)** and **quartz (about 12%)**, followed by pyroxene, mica, and amphibole in smaller amounts. **So, statement I is not correct.**
● **Cinnabar** (mercury sulphide) typically occurs as a vein-filling mineral in regions of geologically recent **volcanic activity** and is commonly deposited by **alkaline hot springs, geysers, and fumaroles. So,** **statement II is correct.**
○ **Cinnabar (HgS, mercury sulfide) is the chief ore of mercury**. When heated, cinnabar releases **mercury vapor**, which is then condensed to obtain liquid mercury.
● Although **feldspar** is widely distributed, **granitic pegmatites** constitute the most important commercial source, as they yield high-purity, large-crystal feldspar required for glass and ceramic industries. **So, statement III is correct.** **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Geography
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements:
I. - Burhi Gandak River joins the Ganga River in
the Patna district of  Bihar.
II. 	 Kosi is a transboundary river that flows
through India and Nepal only.
III. 	 The Mahananda River originates in the
Darjeeling hills and joins the Ganga River in
Bangladesh.
Which of the statements given above is/are
correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The **Burhi Gandak River** is a significant tributary of the Ganga River. It is a **vital water source in** **Bihar**. It flows parallel to the Gandak River. **Burhi Gandak joins Ganga from the left near** **Khagaria. So, statement I is not correct.**
○ The Burhi Gandak is also known as ‘Sikrahna’ in Bihar. ○ It is one of the tributaries that completely flows in Bihar. ○ It originates from Chautarwa Chaur near Bishambharpur in the district of West Champaran. Burhi Gandak passes through the districts of West Champaran, East Champaran, Muzaffarpur, Samastipur, and Khagaria. ○ The last district of Burhi Gandak is Khagaria, where it joins the River Ganga near Gogri Jamalpur**.**
● **Kosi is a transboundary river that flows through China, Nepal, and India.** The Kosi River, an antecedent river**, originates north of Mount Everest in Tibet**, where its main stream, the Arun, rises. In Nepal, it is joined by the Sun Kosi from the west and the Tamur Kosi from the east, forming the Sapt Kosi. The river then flows into India, joining the Ganga on its left bank near Kursela. **So, statement** **II is not correct.**
● **Mahananda is a typical river system consisting of two different streams, one arising from** **the mountainous region of the Himalayas in Nepal**, traverses through the Indian state of Bihar and out falls in the Ganga in the left opposite to Rajmahal in Bihar and is locally named as the river Fulahar. The **other stream, also named as** **Mahananda, rises downhill in Darjeeling in West** **Bengal** and traverses a distance of about 400 kms. through the district of Darjeeling, West Dinajpur and Malda and enters in Bangladesh territory and **ultimately out falls in the Ganga near** **Godagarighat in Bangladesh, just opposite to Lalgola in Murshidabad district of West** **Bengal. So, statement III is correct.** **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Geography
questions.questionText:
With reference to the Dams/Hydro power projects
in India, consider the following information:
Dam/ Hydroelectric Project
Constructed on the River Located In
I. - Ranjit Sagar Beas Punjab
II. - Baglihar Jhelum Jammu and Kashmir
III. - Almatti Krishna Karnataka
IV. - Mullaperiyar Periyar Kerala
In how many of the above rows is the given
information correctly matched?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **The Ranjit Sagar Dam,** also known as the Thien Dam**, is constructed on the river Ravi. It is** **located in Punjab.** The dam is a part of the hydroelectricity project of the state Government of Punjab and was completed in 2001. **So, pair I is not correctly matched.**
● **Baglihar Dam is a run-of-the-river power project on the Chenab River.** The dam, which was commissioned in 2009, is operated by the J&K Power Development Corporation. Baglihar Stage-1 has an installed capacity of 450 megawatts. **So, pair II is not correctly matched.**
● **Almatti Dam is a hydroelectric project on the Krishna River in Karnataka.** It is the main reservoir of the Upper Krishna Irrigation Project. It was built with the primary objectives of supplying irrigation and potable water to adjacent regions, generating hydroelectric energy, and managing flood risks in the area. **So, pair III is correctly matched.**
● **The Mullaperiyar dam was built in 1895 across the Periyar River in Kerala's Idukki district.** It is operated by Tamil Nadu under a lease agreement. It has long been a point of contention with Kerala, citing safety concerns due to its age and seismic vulnerability. Tamil Nadu emphasises its importance for irrigation and drinking water supply to several southern districts. It is one of the oldest dams in India. Its construction began in 1887 and was completed in 1895**. So, pair IV is correctly matched.** **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**
**Knowledge Box**

questions.subject: Geography
questions.questionText:
With reference to the west-flowing rivers in India,
consider the following statements:
I. - The Tapti River drains into the Arabian Sea
through the Gulf of Kutchh.
III. 	 The Mahi River drains into the Arabian Sea
through the Gulf of Khambhat.
III. 	 The Mandovi River falls into the Karanji
Lake.
Which of the statements given above is/are
correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The Tapti is an important westward flowing river. It **originates from Multai in the Betul district of** **Madhya Pradesh.** It is 724 km long and drains an area of 65,145 sq. km. **Nearly 79 per cent of its basin lies in** **Maharashtra, 15 per cent in Madhya Pradesh and the** **remaining 6 per cent in Gujarat**. Its important tributaries are the Suki, the Gomai, the Arunavati and the Aner which joins it from right, and those joining from left are the Vaghur, the Amravati, the Buray, the Panjhra, the Bori, the Girna, the Purna, the Mona and the Sipna. **The river falls into the Arabian Sea through the Gulf of Cambay or Khmabat. So,** **statement I is not correct.**
● **Mahi River** originates from **Vindhyachal Hills in Madhya Pradesh and meets in Gulf of** **Khambhat**. The Mahi basin extends over states of Madhya Pradesh, Rajasthan and Gujarat. The total length of Mahi is 583 km. The river finally falls into the **Gulf of Khambhat in the Arabian Sea.  So,** **statement II is correct.**
● **The Mandovi River** is one of the main west-flowing rivers of Goa. It **rises in the Jamboti Ghats** **and falls into the Arabian Sea. So, statement III is not correct.** **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Geography
questions.microTopic: Straits, Channels, Oceans, Seas, Gulfs, Bays
questions.questionText:
With reference to the region of South East Asia, consider the following statements:
I. - The Philippine Island Arc was formed due to the convergence of the Philippine Sea Plate and the Eurasian Plate.
II. 	 The Phu Khanh Basin is an important sedimentary basin owned by Thailand.
III. 	 The Strait of Singapore is bordered by Indonesia, Malaysia, and Singapore.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The **Philippine Island Arc** is an example of a **convergent plate boundary**. It has formed where the **Philippine Sea Plate subducts beneath the Eurasian Plate**. This tectonic interaction gives rise to a chain of **volcanic islands and deep-sea trenches**. **So, statement I is correct.**
● **The Phu Khanh Basin** is a north-to-south elongated basin in the **Vietnam continental shelf area,**
which comprises a series of Cenozoic sedimentary basins, located within a transitional zone from the continental crust of the Indochina Craton to the oceanic crust of the South China Sea. **So, statement** **II is not correct.**
● The **Strait of Singapore** is a **narrow and busy maritime passage** that connects the **Strait of** **Malacca (to the west)** with the **South China Sea (to the east)**. It is bordered by **Singapore to** **the north**, **Indonesia to the south**, and **Malaysia to the northwest**. This strait is one of the **world’s most important shipping lanes**, facilitating trade between the **Indian Ocean and the** **Pacific Ocean**. **So, statement III is correct.** **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Geography
questions.microTopic: Water in the Atmosphere (Clouds, Condensation, Precipitation, Rainfall, etc.)
questions.questionText:
With reference to the formation of dew, consider the following statements:
I. - It forms under conditions of clear skies, calm air, high relative humidity, and long, cold nights.
II. 	 The Dew point of the air must be above the freezing point.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Dew** is formed when **water vapour** near the ground **condenses into liquid** **droplets** on exposed surfaces as they cool to their **dew point temperature.**
● **Ideal conditions** for dew formation include **clear skies** (allowing strong radiational cooling), **calm or** **light winds** (preventing mixing of air layers), **high relative humidity** (providing ample moisture), and **long, cold nights** (giving enough time for surfaces to cool to the dew point). These conditions enable **water vapour to condense** into droplets on grass, metal surfaces, and soil. **So, statement I is** **correct.**
● Dew forms only when the **dew point is above 0°C**. When the dew point **falls below** freezing, water vapour does **not condense** into liquid; instead, it **sublimates directly into ice crystals, producing** **frost**, not dew. Thus, dew formation **becomes impossible** under sub-freezing dew point conditions. **So, statement II is correct.** **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**
**Knowledge Box**
● **Fog** forms when air containing water vapour cools suddenly, causing condensation on dust particles near the ground.
● **Mist** is similar to fog but contains larger moisture content and forms thicker droplets around nuclei. It is less dense, dissipates faster, and often occurs over mountains where warm air meets a cold surface.
● **Haze** consists of dry particles like dust, smoke, or pollutants, without condensation. It reduces clarity of the atmosphere, but not as much as fog or mist.

questions.subject: Geography
questions.microTopic: Movement of Ocean Water (Tides, Waves, etc.)
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements regarding Tidal Bores:
I. - A tidal bore is a surge of seawater that travels upstream in a river when the incoming tide opposes the river flow.
II. 	 Tidal bores occur frequently along all coasts having a narrow river outlet, irrespective of the tidal range.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● A **tidal bore** represents a **positive surge of water** created when a **rising ocean tide pushes into the mouth of a** **river**, moving upstream **against the natural river flow**. This usually occurs during **spring tides** or periods of **supermoons**, when tidal forces are strongest. The bore often appears as a wall of water traveling inland. Examples include the **Qiantang River bore** in China and the **Pororoca** on the Amazon River. **So, statement I is** **correct.**
● Tidal bores **do not occur along all coasts**, even those with narrow river outlets. Their formation requires a **specific set of conditions**:
○ A **large tidal range** (usually greater than 6 meters). ○ A **funnel-shaped estuary**—wide at the mouth and narrowing inland. ○ A **shallow river channel** with suitable topography.
○ The **alignment of tidal timing** with river flow conditions
● Many rivers around the world do not meet these criteria, which is why tidal bores are a **rare** **phenomenon**, observed only in select locations. **So, statement II is not correct.** **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Agriculture
questions.questionText: The terms ‘Chena’, ‘Roca’, ‘Ray’, and ‘Podu’ are used to refer to which one of the following practices?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Shifting cultivation**, also called **slash-and-burn agriculture**, is a **primitive subsistence farming** **system** practiced on **small patches of land** using **simple tools** like hoe, a dao, and digging sticks, and relying mainly on **family or community labour**.
● It depends entirely on the **monsoon**, the **natural fertility of the soil**, and **favourable** **environmental conditions**.
● In this system, farmers **cut, slash, and burn** a forested patch, mix the ashes with soil, and grow crops such as **maize, yam, potatoes, and cassava**. When the **soil fertility declines**, the land is **abandoned**, and a **new patch** is cleared for cultivation. Since no fertilisers or modern inputs are used, **productivity remains low**.
● Traditionally, the **fallow period ranged from 10 to 20 years**, but in recent times it has **reduced to** **2–5 years**, leading to soil degradation.
● **Chena, Roca, Ray, and Podu** are all various local names for shifting cultivation, also known as "slashand-burn" agriculture, practiced in different parts of the world.
● In India, this practice is known by different names — **Jhum** in Assam and other northeastern states, **Punam Krishi** in Kerala, **Podu** in Andhra Pradesh and Odisha, and **Bewar, Mashan, Penda, and** **Beera** in Madhya Pradesh.
Name of Shifting Cultivation Region
**Ray** **Vietnam**
Tavi Madagascar
Masole Congo (Zaire River Valley)
Fang Equatorial African Countries
Comile Mexico
Milpa Yucatan and Guatemala
**Roka** **Brazil**
Taungya Myanmar
**Chena** **Sri Lanka**
Ladang Java and Indonesia
Tamrai Thailand
Humah Java and Indonesia
**Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer**

questions.subject: Environment
questions.microTopic: Wildlife Conservation (Tiger, Elephant, Lion, Rhino, Crocodile, Vulture, Tortoise, Dolphin, etc.)
questions.questionText:
Consider the following species:
I. - Great Hornbill
II. 	 Sloth Bear
III. 	 Lion-tailed Macaque
IV. 	 Hangul
Which of the above species demonstrates a pronounced ecological dependence on evergreen forest ecosystems?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● Evergreen forests are defined as dense, multilayered forests characterized by broad-leaved trees that retain their foliage year-round.
○ They are typically found in regions where the climate is generally wet and the dry season is very brief.
● **Great Hornbill** is a large bird characterized by a curved yellow bill and a prominent hollow casque. It is **native to South and Southeast Asia** including India. It is listed as  **‘Vulnerable’** by the IUCN and protected under Schedule I of the Wildlife (Protection) Act, 1972. In India, Great Hornbills are found in the Western Ghats and the Himalayas. It is the state bird of Arunachal Pradesh and Kerala.
○ **Great hornbills are found in evergreen and moist deciduous forests, primarily** **inhabiting the canopy of tall trees.** For nesting, they prefer trees that tower above the canopy level.  **So, point I is correct.**
● **Sloth Bears** is one of the four bear species found in India. Sloth bears live in India, Nepal and Sri Lanka. It is listed in the ‘vulnerable’ category by the International Union for Conservation of Nature (IUCN).
○ While the sloth bear **occurs across a variety of habitats including moist tropical forests** it **predominantly inhabits dry deciduous forests, scrublands, and grasslands** across the Indian subcontinent. Its presence in wet evergreen forests is comparatively limited, and it **does** **not exhibit a pronounced or primary ecological dependence on evergreen forest** **ecosystem.** **So, point II is not correct.**
● **Lion-tailed Macaque** (Macaca silenus) is a primate **endemic to rainforests** of the Western Ghats. The Lion-tailed Macaque show a **high frequency of feeding on fruits (59.46 %)**. The lion-tailed macaques are **mostly seen in taller trees**. **So, point III is correct.**
● **Hangul or Kashmiri Stag** is a unique subspecies of red deer native to the Kashmir region. It is the only surviving Asiatic member of the red deer family. It is the state animal of Jammu and Kashmir (UT). The hangul is classified as Critically Endangered by the IUCN Red List. Hangul’s population have constantly declined over the decades, making it largely confined to the 141 square kilometres of **Dachigam National Park.**
○ The hangul lives in dense riverine forests, **high valleys and mountains** of the Kashmir valley and northern Chamba in Himachal Pradesh. It **prefers deciduous woodlands, natural** **grasslands, pastures and meadow**s, and sometimes ventures above the tree-line. It feeds mainly on grasses, herbs, leaves, bark and twigs. The hangul is native to the dense forests and **high-altitude meadows** of the Kashmir Valley. **So, point IV is not correct.** **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Geography
questions.questionText:
With reference to the eye of a tropical cyclone, consider the following statements:
I. - The greater the wind speed in a tropical cyclone, the larger is the eye region.
II. 	 It is the region of heaviest rainfall and strongest convection.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Tropical cyclones** are i**ntense low-pressure systems** forming over warm ocean waters, characterized by strong rotating winds and heavy rainfall. Their structure consists of a calm **central eye,** surrounded by the **eyewall** with maximum winds and intense convection, and **spiral rainbands** **extending outward.** They derive energy from warm sea surface evaporation and latent heat release.
● The **eye of a tropical cyclone** is the **central calm region** surrounded by the eyewall, which contains the most intense winds, rainfall, and convective activity. The eye forms due to **strong rotational forces** causing sinking air in the center.
● As a **cyclone intensifies, wind speeds increase** and centripetal force strengthens, causing the **eye** **to contract.** Thus, **stronger cyclones** have **smaller, tighter eyes (known as “pin-hole eyes”).** Weaker cyclones have broader eyes. **So, statement I is not correct.**
● The **eye** is actually the **calmest** and **warmest** part of the cyclone, with **clear skies** and **very little** **rainfall.** The **heaviest rainfall** and **strongest winds** occur in the **eyewall**, the ring of deep convective clouds surrounding the eye. **So, statement II is not correct.** **Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Geography
questions.questionText: Which of the following sequences correctly represents the typical methane yield potential of the given feedstocks, arranged from highest to lowest?

options.a: Fats and oils > Food waste > Cattle manure > Wheat straw
options.b: Cattle manure > Wheat straw > Food waste > Fats and oils
options.c: Wheat straw > Cattle manure > Fats and oils > Food waste
options.d: Food waste > Fats and oils > Wheat straw > Cattle manure

questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Methane yield from different biomass feedstocks** depends largely on their **energy density** and **chemical composition**, particularly the ease with which their organic matter can be decomposed by **anaerobic microorganisms** during biogas production.
○ **Fats and Oils (Lipids):** These are the most **energy-dense** organic materials and generate the **highest methane yield per unit mass**, as they are readily broken down under anaerobic conditions. ○ **Food Waste (mixed carbohydrates, proteins, and fats):** Food waste generally shows a **high methane potential** due to the presence of easily degradable carbohydrates and proteins, along with some fats. ○ **Cattle Manure:** Although available in large quantities, manure has already undergone **partial** **digestion in animals**, reducing the amount of readily available organic energy and thus its methane yield. ○ **Wheat Straw (lignocellulosic material):** Dominated by **cellulose, hemicellulose, and** **lignin**, with lignin being highly resistant to microbial action, wheat straw yields the **lowest** **methane output** among these feedstocks. **So, the correct sequence representing the typical methane yield potential of the given feedstocks,** **arranged from highest to lowest is:  Fats and oils > Food waste > Cattle manure > Wheat straw.** **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Agriculture
questions.questionText:
Consider the following pairs:
Crop Native to
l. Pineapple India
II. - Coffee Ethiopia
III. - Cashew Brazil
IV. - Red Sanders Sri Lanka
How many of the pairs given above are correctly
matched?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The **pineapple** crop is native to **South America**, specifically the region of southern **Brazil** and **Paraguay**. From its origin, it spread throughout the Americas and was later introduced to other **tropical and subtropical regions** around the world by explorers and traders.  **So, pair I is not** **correctly matched.**
● **Coffee originated in Ethiopia,** the birthplace of the crop, before spreading to Yemen, the Middle East, and later to Europe and the Americas. **So, pair II is correctly matched.**
● The **cashew** crop is native to **Brazil** in northeastern South America. It was **introduced** to other parts of the world, including **India**, **by Portuguese** colonists centuries ago. While it originated in Brazil, cashews are now a **major crop in countries like India, Vietnam**, and several **West African** **nations**. **So, pair III is correctly matched.**
● **Red sanders** is native to a narrow area of the **Southern Eastern Ghats in the Indian state of** **Andhra Pradesh.** Specifically, it is endemic to the forests of the **Seshachalam**, **Veligonda**, **Lankamala**, and **Palakonda** Hill ranges in districts like **Kadapa**, **Nellore**, **Chittoor**, and **Prakasham**. **So, pair IV is not correctly matched.** **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Geography
questions.microTopic: Demography, Population, Census (Literacy, Sex Ratio, etc.)
questions.questionText:
Which one of the following States the Total
Fertility Rate (TFR) below the replacement level
of fertility?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● India’s population in April 2025 is estimated at **146.39 crore**, the highest globally. It is projected to peak at **170 crore** in the early 2060s before gradually declining. **Life expectancy** is projected at **71 years** **for men** and **74 years for women**.
● The **Total Fertility Rate (TFR)** has dropped to **2.0**, below the **replacement level of 2.1** as per the fifth round of the **National Family Health Survey (NFHS-5).**
● There are now only five states that have TFR above the replacement level of fertility: **Bihar (2.98),** **Meghalaya (2.91), Uttar Pradesh (2.35), Jharkhand (2.26) and Manipur (**2.17).
● As per NFHS-5,  the **total fertility rate (TFR) in Gujarat is 1.9** children per woman. **Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Geography
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements with reference
to the summer solstice:
I. - During the Summer Solstice, the Earth is
farthest from the Sun.
II. 	 In the Northern Hemisphere, the region
beyond the Arctic Circle experiences 24
hours of continuous daylight.
Which of the statements given above is/are
correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The **summer solstice** occurs around **21st June**, when the **Northern** **Hemisphere** is maximally tilted toward the Sun. The Sun’s rays fall directly on the **Tropic of Cancer (23.5°N),** resulting in the longest day and shortest night in the Northern Hemisphere.
● **The Earth is farthest from the sun** **during aphelion, which occurs in** **early July, about two weeks after the June solstice. So, statement I is not correct.**
● During the **summer solstice,** the **North Pole** is tilted at its maximum angle (23.5°) toward the Sun. Consequently, the entire region beyond the Arctic Circle (66.5° N) lies within the zone of **continuous** **illumination and experiences 24 hours of daylight,** a phenomenon known as the **midnight sun.** **So, statement II is correct.** **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**
**Knowledge Box**
● **The Winter Solstice** marks the **shortest day and the longest night** of the year. It occurs when one of the **Earth’s poles is tilted farthest away from the Sun**, a result of the Earth’s axial tilt of 23.4°. During this time, the Sun’s apparent path across the sky is at its lowest altitude, resulting in reduced daylight hours. The Winter Solstice **occurs around December 21 or 22 in the Northern Hemisphere and around** **June 20 or 21 in the Southern Hemisphere.**
● The **equinoxes** are **astronomical events** that occur **twice a year**, in **March** and **September**, when the **Sun is directly above the Equator at noon**, resulting in **nearly equal day and night** across the globe. During an equinox, both the **Northern** **and Southern Hemispheres** receive **equal sunlight**, as the Earth’s **axial tilt** is aligned neither toward nor away from the Sun. The **Vernal (Spring) Equinox**, occurring around **March 19–21**, marks the onset of **spring in the Northern** **Hemisphere** and **autumn in the Southern Hemisphere**. The **Autumnal** **Equinox**, around **September 21–24**, signals **autumn in the Northern** **Hemisphere** and **spring in the Southern Hemisphere**.

questions.subject: Environment
questions.microTopic: Air, Water, Noise Pollution, SLCPs, etc.
questions.questionText:
With reference to the geographical factors behind recurrent winter air pollution in Delhi, consider the following statements:
I. - Delhi’s location within the Indo-Gangetic Plain creates a basin-like topography that restricts horizontal air circulation.
II. 	 During late autumn, temperature inversion increases the atmospheric mixing height, preventing pollutants from dispersing upward.
III.	 The presence of the Himalayas to the north and the Aravalli Hills to the southwest limits wind flow, reducing pollutant dispersion.
Which of the statements given above are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Delhi’s acute winter air pollution** episodes result from an **interplay of** **emissions** (stubble burning, vehicular and industrial pollutants, dust) and **geographical-meteorological factors** that limit pollutant dispersion.
● Delhi lies in the **Indo-Gangetic Plain (IGP)** — a broad, low, relatively flat tract that behaves like a shallow bowl. This **basin-like topography** restricts horizontal advection of air masses at low levels; with **weak winds in post-monsoon months,** pollutants emitted locally or advected from neighbouring states remain confined near the surface, leading to elevated concentrations. **So,** **statement I is correct.**
● Contrary to the statement, **temperature inversion reduces the atmospheric mixing height**; it does not increase it. Inversion means a **warm layer overlies cooler near-surface air**, creating a stable lid that suppresses vertical motion. This lowers the depth of the **well-mixed layer (mixing** **height)** and prevents pollutants from rising and dispersing, thereby intensifying surface pollution. **So,** **statement II is not correct.**
● The **Himalayas** to the north and the **Aravalli range** to the southwest act as **natural barriers** that modify regional wind patterns. These uplands **limit the inflow of cleaner air** and **impede the** **horizontal flushing** of pollutants. Combined with the **flat IGP** and **seasonal weak winds,** these barriers further reduce pollutant dispersion and worsen winter-time smog in Delhi. **So, statement III** **is correct.** **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Science & Technology
questions.questionText: In astronomical observations, the term ‘red shift’ refers to:

options.a: Decrease in the intensity of light due to interstellar dust
options.b: Shift of light towards longer wavelengths when galaxies move away
options.c: Light bending due to massive celestial bodies
options.d: Sudden brightening of a star due to an explosion

questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Red shift** is a key concept in **astronomy** referring to the phenomenon where the **wavelength** **of** **light** **is** **stretched**, making the light appear **shifted toward the red end** of the electromagnetic spectrum.
● This idea is explained using the **Doppler effect**, discovered by **Christian Andreas Doppler**. The Doppler effect describes how the **frequency of sound waves changes** when the **sound source and** **the observer move relative to each other**. When a sound source **approaches**, the **frequency** **increases**, and when it **recedes**, the **frequency decreases**. Everyday examples include **ambulance** **sirens**, **train whistles**, and **racing car engines** where the pitch changes as they pass by.
● Since **light also behaves as a wave**, light from objects moving relative to us undergoes a **similar shift**. In **1929**, **Edwin Hubble** discovered the **expansion of the Universe**, leading to the observation that **most galaxies are moving away from us**. Their light, therefore, becomes **red-shifted**, meaning its **wavelength becomes longer**.
● A **blue shift** is the **displacement of light** to a **shorter wavelength** (and **higher frequency**), caused by an **object moving toward an observer**. This is the **opposite** of a **redshift**, which occurs when an **object moves away**.
● **Gravitational lensin**g occurs when a massive celestial body — such as a galaxy cluster — causes a sufficient curvature of spacetime for the path of light around it to be visibly bent, as if by a lens. The body causing the light to curve is accordingly called a gravitational lens.
● A sudden brightening of a star due to an explosion is called a **nova or a supernova.** **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Geography
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements:
**Statement I:** Countries with a Mediterranean climate are net importers of dairy products.
**Statement II:** The Mediterranean region does not favour the growth of nutritious grasses.
Which one of the following is correct in respect of the above statements?

options.a: Both Statement I and Statement II are correct and Statement II explains Statement I
options.b: Both Statement I and Statement II are correct but Statement II does **not** explain Statement I
options.c: Statement I is correct but Statement II is incorrect
options.d: Statement I is incorrect but Statement II is correct

questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The **Mediterranean Climate**, also known as the **Warm Temperate** **Western Margin Climate** or **Warm Temperate West Coast** **Climate**, occurs on the **western** **sides of continents between 30°** **and** **45°** **latitudes** in both hemispheres. It arises due to the seasonal shifting of global wind belts, giving the region hot, dry summers and cool, wet winters.
● This climate is best developed in **central Chile**, with other major regions including **California (San** **Francisco)**, **southwestern South Africa (Cape Town)**, and **southern and southwestern** **Australia**. The **Mediterranean Sea** region gives its name to this climatic type.
● Summers are **hot, dry, cloudless**, and influenced by **offshore trade winds**, which block rainfall. Strong winds from inland deserts increase the risk of wildfires. **Winters receive most of the rainfall due** **to the on-shore Westerlies,** bringing cyclonic rain that is heavy but infrequent, often causing torrential downpours and floods in mountainous areas.
● Mediterranean climate countries are **net exporters of citrus fruits** but **net importers of dairy** **products** due to their limited cattle rearing capabilities.  **So, statement I is correct.**
○ Their **clear summer skies, scenic landscapes, and pleasant climate** make them major **tourist destinations**, and the European Mediterranean, with its **ancient cities and famous** **resorts**, attracts **millions of visitors year-round**.
● **Grasses** grow poorly because rain comes in the cool season, making them **wiry and unsuitable for** **grazing**, which is why **cattle rearing is limited** in Mediterranean regions. **So, statement II is** **correct and correctly explains statement I.**
● **Local winds** are prominent: the **Sirocco** is a hot, dusty wind blowing from the Sahara, sometimes causing “blood rain” due to red dust; the **Mistral** is a cold, violent north wind funneled through the Rhone Valley; similar cold winds include the **Bora, Tramontane, and Gregale.** **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Geography
questions.questionText:
With reference to the groundwater use in India, consider the following statements:
**Statement I:** More than 50% of India’s groundwater blocks are classified as over-exploited. **Statement-II:** India has the largest area under groundwater irrigation in the world.
Which one of the following is correct in respect of the above statements?

options.a: Both Statement I and Statement II are correct and Statement II explains Statement I
options.b: Both Statement I and Statement II are correct but Statement II does **not** explain Statement I
options.c: Statement I is correct but Statement II is incorrect
options.d: Statement I is incorrect but Statement II is correct

questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The **Central Ground Water Board (CGWB)** periodically assesses India’s **Dynamic Ground** **Water Resources** using **assessment units** such as blocks, talukas, mandals, tehsils, and firkas not river basins. Since **2022**, assessments have been conducted **annually**.
● According to the **Central Groundwater Board (CGWB)** report, Dynamic Groundwater Resources of India 2022, **14%** of India’s **7,089 groundwater assessment units** are **over-exploited**, and **4%** fall in the **critical** category. Groundwater assessment units refer to blocks, talukas or mandals used for groundwater evaluation. **So, statement I is not correct.**
● **Over-exploited units** are those where **extraction exceeds annual recharge**, while **critical units** have a **stage of extraction between 90–100%**. India’s **overall extraction level is 60.08%.**
● Extraction is **more than 100%** in **Punjab, Haryana, Rajasthan**, and the UTs of **Dadra & Nagar** **Haveli, Daman & Diu**, placing them in the **over-exploited range**. **Delhi**, with **98.16%** extraction, falls in the **critical** category.
● **India** is the **largest user of groundwater globally** and has the largest area irrigated by groundwater. **So, statement II is correct.**
○ Agriculture remains the **largest consumer**, using **87% (208.49 bcm)** of all extracted groundwater, while domestic and industrial sectors account for **13%**.
● Between 2017 and 2024, annual groundwater recharge increased by 15 BCM (billion cubic meters), while extraction declined by 3 BCM, indicating slight improvement.
● Groundwater extraction categories show that most states fall in the **Safe (<70%)** range, while **Punjab,** **Rajasthan, Dadra & Nagar Haveli, Daman & Diu, Haryana, and Delhi** remain **Over-exploited** **(>100%)**. No state is currently in the **Critical (90–100%)** category. **So, statement-I is incorrect but statement-II is correct.** **Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.**
**Knowledge Box**
**Key Facts About the Central Groundwater Board:**
● The **Central Ground Water Board (CGWB)**, under the **Ministry of Jal Shakti**, is the apex body for **exploring, monitoring, assessing, and managing** **groundwater** in India.
● Formed in **1970** and expanded in **1972** after merging with the Ground Water Wing of the GSI, it provides scientific inputs for groundwater management and implements **artificial recharge** and **rainwater harvesting** measures.
● Its regulatory arm, the **Central Ground Water Authority (CGWA)**, was set up under the **Environment Protection Act, 1986** to control groundwater extraction.
● CGWB also publishes key scientific documents like **hydrogeological reports,** **groundwater yearbooks, and atlases**.

questions.subject: Geography
questions.questionText:
With reference to migration in India, consider the following statements:
I. - Domestic migrants constitute more than onefourth of India’s total population.
II. 	 Employment is the single largest reason for intra-state migration in India.
Which of the statements given above is/ are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The **Economic Advisory Council to the Prime Minister (EAC-PM)** reports that **domestic** **migration in India is slowing**, with the total number of migrants falling by **11.78%** since the Census 2011.
● As of **2023**, India has an estimated **40.20 crore migrants**, compared to **45.57 crore** in 2011. Consequently, the **migration rate** has dropped from **37.64% (2011)** to **28.88%** of the population. **So, statement I is correct.**
●  Major **origin districts** cluster around metropolitan zones such as **Delhi, Mumbai, Chennai,** **Bengaluru, and Kolkata**.
● Top **destination states** now include **Uttar Pradesh, Maharashtra, Madhya Pradesh, West** **Bengal, and Rajasthan**, with West Bengal and Rajasthan emerging as new entrants.
● Intra-state movement accounted for almost 88% of all internal migration. **Majority** (70%) of **intra-state** **migration** was due to reasons of **marriage and family.**  **So, statement II is not correct.**
● According to the **Census 2011**, rural-to-urban migration in India is driven by several social and economic factors.
○ **Work and employment opportunities** motivate **24%** of migrants to shift to urban areas. ○ Other contributors include people who **moved after birth** (**5.5%**), those migrating for **education** (**2%**), and a mix of **other reasons,** such as health, housing, and social security, making up **12%**.
● The **number of international migrants living in India has been steadily declining** over the past three decades.
● As per the EAC-PM Working Paper (2024) states showing maximum growth in migrant arrivals include **West Bengal, Rajasthan, and Karnataka**, while **Maharashtra and Andhra Pradesh** show a decline in their share.
● **Mumbai, Bengaluru Urban, Howrah, Central Delhi, and Hyderabad** attract the highest inflows; **Valsad, Chittoor, Paschim Bardhaman, Agra, Guntur, Villupuram, and Saharsa** are major origin districts. **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Geography
questions.questionText:
Consider the following ports:
I. - Mundra Port
II. 	 Kandla Port
III. 	 Jawaharlal Nehru Port
IV. 	 Mormugao Port
How many of the above are part of the IndiaMiddle East-Europe Economic Corridor (IMEC)?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The **India–Middle East–Europe Economic Corridor (IMEC)** aims to upgrade maritime connectivity between **India and the Arabian Peninsula** and create a high-speed rail network linking **UAE–Saudi Arabia–Jordan (Israel)**, with onward access to Europe.
● It also envisions a **clean hydrogen pipeline, an electricity cable, an undersea digital cable**, and the consolidation of port infrastructure.
● The corridor was launched at the **G-20 Summit 2023** under favourable geopolitical conditions shaped by the **Abraham Accords** and strengthened India–UAE–Saudi ties, as well as the I2U2 framework (India, Israel, UAE, U.S.).
● IMEC is supported by several major global economies and institutions, including **Saudi Arabia, India, the UAE, the** **United States, France, Italy, Germany**, and the **European Union**, reflecting a broad international commitment to building a resilient and interconnected global trade architecture.
● It will operate through two major segments: an **East** **Corridor** linking India with the Arabian Gulf and a **Northern Corridor** connecting the Arabian Gulf to Europe.
● While railways form the backbone of the project, a **ship-to-rail transit system** will complement existing maritime and road networks through key regional hub ports.
● Under IMEC, connectivity between Indian ports such as **Mundra, Kandla and Jawaharlal Nehru** **Port** and Gulf ports including **Fujairah, Jebel Ali and Khalifa (UAE)** and **Dammam and Ras Al** **Khair (Saudi Arabia)** will be strengthened, alongside improvements in **port efficiency** and capacity to handle high-technology cargo such as electronics, semiconductors, pharmaceuticals, solar modules, batteries, turbines and machinery components. **So, points I, II, and III are correct.**
● Currently, **Mormugao port is not included** in the  India-Middle East-Europe Economic Corridor (IMEC) project.
○ Mormugao Port, established in 1885 in Goa, is a key maritime hub, originally developed for iron ore exports. Designated a Major Port in 1964, it has grown significantly, especially during peak global demand for iron ore.
● The corridor also emphasises **greenfield port development**, creation of **mega-integrated logistics** **hubs**, and the **greening of ports** for future sustainability. Another key pillar is completing missing rail links between five major UAE–Saudi ports and **Haifa (Israel)** to enable seamless **cross-border rail** **transport**.
● From Haifa, the corridor aims to enhance maritime connectivity to major European ports such as **Piraeus (Greece)**, **Gioia Tauro (Italy)** and **Marseille (France)**, completing a high-efficiency multimodal trade route across three regions. **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Geography
questions.microTopic: Straits, Channels, Oceans, Seas, Gulfs, Bays
questions.questionText: Which of the following statements best describes the difference between a Strait and an Isthmus?

options.a: Strait is a natural water channel while isthmus is an artificial channel connecting two water bodies
options.b: A Strait is a narrow body of water connecting two larger water bodies, whereas an Isthmus is a narrow strip of land connecting two larger landmasses
options.c: A Strait has a wider opening to the sea, while an Isthmus has a narrow mouth and extends deeper inland

questions.explanationMarkdown:
● A **Strait** is a **narrow water passage** that connects two larger bodies of water and separates two landmasses, often serving as key routes for maritime navigation. Examples include the **Strait of Gibraltar** (connecting the Atlantic Ocean and the Mediterranean Sea) and the **Strait of** **Malacca** (linking the Indian Ocean with the Pacific Ocean).
● An **Isthmus**, by contrast, is a **narrow strip of land** that connects two larger landmasses while separating two water bodies. Famous examples are the **Isthmus of Panama** (linking North and South America) and the **Isthmus of Suez** (connecting Africa and Asia). **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Geography
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements with regard to Ekman Transport:
I. - It refers to the net movement of surface water at about 90° to the direction of the prevailing wind.
II. 	 Coastal upwelling occurs where Ekman transport moves surface waters toward the coast.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● Ekman Transport occurs due to the **combined action of wind stress** **and the Coriolis force**. When the wind blows over the ocean surface, friction causes the upper layer of water to move. Because of Earth’s rotation, this movement is deflected **to the** **right in the Northern Hemisphere** and **to the left in the Southern** **Hemisphere**. As a result, the **net** **transport of the surface layer** **occurs roughly 90°** to the wind direction. **So,** **statement**
**I** **is** **correct.**
● **Coastal upwelling** actually occurs where **Ekman Transport moves** **surface waters away from the** **coast**. When winds blow parallel to the coastline and the surface water is deflected offshore, it creates a void that is filled by **cold, nutrient-rich water** **rising from deeper layers**. This process brings essential nutrients like nitrates and phosphates into the sunlit surface zone, stimulating **phytoplankton growth**. In contrast, when **Ekman Transport moves surface waters toward the coast**, the water piles up and **sinks**, resulting in **coastal downwelling**, which suppresses productivity because nutrient exchange from deeper waters is blocked. **So, statement II is not correct.** **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Geography
questions.questionText:
Which of the following are examples of biological weathering?
I. - Repeated heating and cooling, causing rock exfoliation in deserts
II. 	 Dissolution of limestone by carbonic acid in rainwater
III. 	 Growth of plant roots into rock cracks leading to fragmentation
IV. 	 Secretion of organic acids by organisms causing rock disintegration
Select the correct answer using the code given below:
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● Weathering is the process by which rocks are broken down into smaller particles through **physical,** **chemical, or biological means**. Biological weathering refers to the process by which living organisms, such as plants, animals, and humans, contribute to the breakdown of rocks and minerals.
● In arid regions, rocks experience extreme temperature differences between day and night. The outer layers expand and contract repeatedly, leading to **exfoliation or peeling** of rock surfaces. No living organism is involved in this process. It is an example of **physical (mechanical) weathering**, not biological. **So, point I is not correct.**
● Carbon dioxide in the atmosphere dissolves in rainwater, forming weak **carbonic acid**, which reacts with calcium carbonate in limestone to produce soluble calcium bicarbonate. This process forms features like **karst landscapes, caves, and sinkholes**, but it does not involve any biological activity. This is an example of **chemical weathering**. **So, point II is not correct.**
● As **plant roots grow and expand** within cracks or joints of rocks, they exert **tremendous physical** **pressure**, widening the cracks and eventually breaking the rock apart. It is an example of biological weathering. **So, point III is correct.**
● Certain **microorganisms, lichens, mosses, and algae** produce **organic acids** (like humic and oxalic acids) that chemically react with rock minerals, leading to their **decomposition**. This is an example of **biological weathering. So, point IV is correct.** **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Geography
questions.questionText:
Consider the following pairs:
River Drains Into
I. - Yangtze East China Sea
II. - Euphretes Persian Gulf
III. - Sein Mediterranean Sea
IV. - St. Lawrence Atlantic Ocean
How many of the pairs given above are correctly matched?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The **Yangtze River**, also known as the **Chang Jiang**, is the **longest river in Asia** and the **third-** **longest in the world**. It flows entirely within China, originating from the **Tibetan Plateau** and moving eastward across central China before **draining into the East China Sea** near **Shanghai**. It plays a crucial role in Chinese agriculture, transport, and hydropower. Recently, China has dismantled 300 dams and shut down most small hydropower stations on a major Yangtze River tributary to help restore fish habitats and river health. **So, pair I is correctly matched.**
● The **Euphrates River**, along with the **Tigris**, forms the **Tigris-Euphrates river system**, one of the cradles of ancient Mesopotamian civilization. Originating from the mountains of **eastern Turkey**, it flows through **Syria and Iraq**, eventually **joining the Tigris** to form the **Shatt al-Arab**, which **empties into the Persian Gulf**. In recent years Tigris and Euphrates Rivers have seen their levels drop by up to 27 percent due to poor rainfall and upstream water restrictions. **So, pair II is correctly** **matched.**
● The **Seine River** does **not** drain into the **Mediterranean Sea**. Instead, it **flows northwest through** **Paris** and **empties into the English Channel**. The Seine is a major river of **northern France**, famous for its navigability. Recently, after more than a century, swimmers returned to the River Seine, marking the first official reopening of the iconic waterway for public bathing since 1923. **So, pair III is** **not correctly matched.**
● The **St. Lawrence River** is a major North American river that serves as a **natural outlet of the** **Great Lakes**. It flows in a **northeast direction**, forming part of the **boundary between Canada** **and the United States**, and **drains into the Atlantic Ocean** through the **Gulf of St. Lawrence**. Recently, the Saint Lawrence River has reached abnormally low levels in Quebec (Canada). **So, pair IV** **is correctly matched.** **So, only three of the above pairs are correctly matched.** **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer**.

questions.subject: Geography
questions.questionText:
Consider the following characteristics:
I. 	 Alternate wet and dry seasons
II. 	 Rains occur mainly in summer
III.	 Found in parts of Northern Karnataka, Southern Maharashtra, and Telangana
Which one of the following climates is best described above?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The **Savanna Climate**, also known as the **Tropical Wet and Dry Climate** or **Sudan Climate**, is characterized by **alternate wet and dry seasons** similar to the monsoon type but with **less annual rainfall**.
● There are **only two distinct seasons,** **summer and winter**, with rain occurring in the summer months.
● This climate is confined within the **tropical belt** and is best developed in **Sudan**, hence the name. It forms a **transitional zone** between the **equatorial rainforests** and the **tropical deserts**.
○ The **African Savanna** stretches across **West Africa**, curves into **East Africa**, and extends to the **southern parts north of the Tropic of Capricorn**. ○ In **South America**, the **llanos of the Orinoco Basin** (north of the equator) and the **campos** **of the Brazilian Highlands** (south of the equator) represent savanna regions. ○ In **Australia**, savannas extend across the **north**, just below the monsoon strip, while in **India**, areas of **northern Karnataka, southern Maharashtra, and Telangana** display savanna-like features modified by **irrigation and cultivation**.
● The **mean annual rainfall** ranges between **80–160 cm**, decreasing with distance from the equator. In the **Northern Hemisphere**, rains occur between **May and September**, while in the **Southern** **Hemisphere**, they fall between **October and March**. The **mean annual temperature** exceeds **18°C**, generally fluctuating between **20°C and 32°C**. The **hottest months** are just before the rainy season, not at the time of the highest sun.
● The **Trade Winds** dominate the region, bringing rainfall to coastal areas. These winds are strongest in the summer when the **Inter-Tropical Convergence Zone (ITCZ)** shifts, but they lose moisture as they move inland, causing rainfall to decrease from **east to west**.
● In **West Africa**, the **Northeast Trade winds** blow dry air from the **Sahara Desert** toward the coast. The alternating **onshore winds in summer** and **offshore winds in winter** explain the **seasonal wet and dry pattern** of this climate. **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**
**Knowledge Box**
**Key Facts About Other Climate Patterns:**
● The **Tropical Rainforest Climate** occurs near the **Equator** and is **hot and wet** **throughout the year**. It is found in regions like **Brazil (Amazon Basin)**, **Costa** **Rica**, **Kenya**, and **Borneo**.
○ Rainfall occurs **almost daily**, exceeding **200 cm annually**, and there are **no** **distinct seasons**. Temperatures remain **high and constant**, usually between **26°C and 29°C**, with an annual range of only **1–2°C**. ○ The combination of **intense heat and heavy rainfall** creates a **humid** **environment**, supporting **dense evergreen forests** with rich biodiversity. This climate is marked by **high humidity**, **abundant rainfall**, and **uniform** **temperatures** throughout the year.
● The **Steppe Climate**, also called the **Temperate Continental** or **Temperate** **Grassland Climate**, occurs in the **interiors of continents** within the **mid-** **latitudes**, where **rain-bearing winds rarely reach**. It is found in regions like the **Eurasian Steppes**, **North American Prairies**, **Argentinian Pampas**, **Hungarian** **Puszta**, **Australian Downs**, and **South African Velds**.
○ The climate is **continental**, with **hot summers** and **cold winters**. Average rainfall is **25–75 cm per year**, mostly in **late spring and early summer**, and decreases toward the interior. The **Chinook winds** in North America warm the Prairies in winter, melting snow and aiding early farming. ○ **Vegetation** mainly consists of **short, nutritious grasses**, making these regions ideal for **wheat cultivation** and **ranching**. Trees are scarce due to low rainfall. The **chernozem (black soil)** in the Steppes and Prairies supports **mechanized farming**, earning them the title **“granaries of the world.”**
○ Economically, these areas are centers of **wheat, maize, and cattle** **production**. The **Prairies and Pampas** focus on **grain and beef exports**, while the **Downs and Canterbury** specialize in **sheep rearing** and **wool** **production**.
● The **Warm Temperate Eastern Margin Climate** or **China Type Climate** occurs on the **east coasts of continents** between **20°–35° latitudes** in both hemispheres. It has **hot, wet summers** and **cool, dry winters**, with rainfall between **60–150 cm** and frequent **typhoons or hurricanes**.
○ The **China Type** (East Asia) shows strong monsoonal influence, the **Gulf Type** (southeastern USA) has mild monsoon effects and hurricanes, and the **Natal** **Type** (South Africa) has evenly distributed rainfall due to maritime winds. ○ **Vegetation** includes **evergreen and deciduous forests** with trees like **oak,** **camphor, and eucalyptus**. The climate supports **maize (Corn Belt, USA)**, **cotton (Gulf States)**, **tobacco (Virginia)**, and **citrus fruits**, making it one of the most productive agricultural regions in the world.

questions.subject: Geography
questions.questionText: Which of the following is the correct reason for the low frequency of landslides in the Western Ghats compared to the Himalayas?

options.a: The Western Ghats are ancient, geologically stable block mountains, while the Himalayas are young, tectonically active fold mountains.
options.b: The Western Ghats consist mainly of hard, compact rocks such as basalt and gneiss, compared to the weak sedimentary rocks of the Himalayas.
options.c: The Western Ghats experience limited seismic activity, while the Himalayas experience frequent earthquakes.

questions.explanationMarkdown:
● Landslides include all the processes by which weathered and unweathered earth materials move downslope under the influence of gravity. Although landslides are more common in mountainous and hilly areas, they also occur underwater and in coastal as well as onshore regions. A landslide can be triggered by the combination of many factors or a single factor. Landslides are often triggered by earthquakes, volcanic eruptions or heavy rainfall.
● The **Western Ghats** are part of the **ancient Peninsular Block**, composed of stable and hard crystalline rocks formed millions of years ago. These mountains have remained largely free from tectonic upheavals in recent times. In contrast, the **Himalayas** are **young fold mountains** still rising due to the collision of the Indian and Eurasian plates. This tectonic instability creates frequent **earthquakes,** **thrust faults, and slope failures**, making the Himalayas highly prone to landslides. **So, option (a) is** **correct.**
● The **Western Ghats** are composed largely of **igneous and metamorphic rocks** like basalt (Deccan Traps), gneiss, and charnockite. These rocks are **dense, compact, and resistant to weathering and** **erosion**, providing slope stability. In contrast, the **Himalayas** consist mainly of **young sedimentary** **and metamorphic rocks**, which are **weak, fractured, and loosely compacted**. These rocks easily crumble under rainfall or seismic stress, making landslides more frequent there.  **So, option (b) is** **correct.**
● The **Himalayas** lie in one of the most **tectonically active zones** in the world, where the Indian plate continues to collide with the Eurasian plate, generating **frequent earthquakes and crustal** **movements**. These seismic tremors destabilize slopes and trigger numerous landslides. The **Western** **Ghats**, in contrast, lie in the **stable Peninsular Shield**, where seismic activity is minimal and of low magnitude. Hence, the Western Ghats have lower frequency of landslides compared to the Himalayas. **So, option (c) is correct.** **So, all the given factors collectively explain the high incidence of landslides in the Himalayas and** **their relative rarity in the Western Ghats.** **Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Geography
questions.questionText:
Consider the following pairs:
Drainage Pattern Description
I. - Obsequent rivers
Tributaries flowing in the opposite direction to its master consequent stream
II. - Subsequent rivers
Streams joining the master stream at right angles
III. - Resequent rivers
Streams that do not follow the regional slope
IV. - Consequent rivers
Streams that follow the general direction of slope
How many of the pairs given above are correctly matched?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Obsequent streams** are **tributaries** **that flow opposite to the direction of** **the master consequent river**. These usually form during later stages of landscape development when erosional processes modify slopes and cause reversal of local drainage. They are not aligned at right angles but often flow directly against the main consequent stream.  **So, pair I is correctly** **matched.**
● **Subsequent rivers** develop **after** the formation of the main consequent drainage. They originate along **zones of weaker rock or structural lines such as faults or folds**, often on the flanks of **anticlines** or along valleys carved by erosion. They characteristically **flow nearly** **at right angles** to the master consequent river, exploiting zones of weakness created by geological structures. **So, pair II is correctly matched.**
● **Insequent rivers** show **no relation to regional slope or geological structure**; they follow random paths formed by irregular erosion. In contrast, **resequent rivers** flow in the **same direction as the** **original consequent stream**, but at **lower topographic levels** formed later in the landscape’s evolution.  **So, pair III is not correctly matched.**
● **Consequent rivers** are the **first streams to develop on newly uplifted land or surfaces**, and their flow direction is determined entirely by the **initial slope of the terrain**. They are “primary” streams in the drainage evolution sequence. Over time, they may develop tributaries and a drainage network influenced by the underlying topography.  **So, pair IV is correctly matched.** **So, only three of the above pairs are correctly matched.** **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer**.

questions.subject: Geography
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements with reference to the Tibetan Plateau:
I. - It is the highest plateau in the world, often referred to as the “Roof of the World.”
II. 	 The Changthang Plateau in India is an extension of this Plateau.
III. 	 The Manasarovar, Namtso, and Yamdrok lakes are located on this Plateau.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The **Tibetan Plateau** is one of the most striking physiographic features on Earth. Located in Central Asia, it is the **largest and highest plateau in the world**. Bordered by the **Himalayas in the south** and the **Kunlun Mountains in the north**, it plays a vital role in shaping Asia’s climate and hydrology.
● The Tibetan Plateau has an **average elevation exceeding 4,500 meters (about 15,000 feet)** above sea level, making it the **highest and largest plateau** on Earth. Because of its vast height and extent, it is popularly known as the **“Roof of the World.”** It was formed due to the **collision of the Indian** **and Eurasian tectonic plates**, which uplifted the landmass over millions of years. The plateau’s altitude and geological activity significantly influence regional weather patterns and global climate systems. **So,** **statement I is correct.**
● The **Changthang Plateau** (Chang Thang refers to High Plateau), located in **Ladakh**, India, is a striking high-altitude expanse that stretches into northern Tibet. The Changthang Plateau forms a significant **part** **of the Tibetan Plateau,** a major geological and climatic feature of Central Asia. It is characterized by vast, flat expanses interspersed with salt flats, sand dunes, and rugged mountain ranges. **So, statement** **II is correct.**
● The **Tibetan Plateau** hosts several high-altitude lakes, and **Manasarovar, Namtso, and Yamdrok** are among the most prominent. **So, statement III is correct.**
○ **Lake Manasarovar** lies near **Mount Kailash** in southwestern Tibet and holds great religious significance for Hindus, Buddhists, Jains, and Bon followers. ○ **Namtso Lake** (meaning “Heavenly Lake”) is one of the largest saltwater lakes in Tibet, located north of Lhasa. ○ **Yamdrok Lake**, with its turquoise waters, lies to the south of Lhasa and is also revered in Tibetan culture. **Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.**
